item.title: Guided Meditation: Goodness and Equanimity
item.description:
AudioDharma talk by Matthew Brensilver.
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/25633
item.itunes:author: Matthew Brensilver
item.itunes:summary: AudioDharma talk by Matthew Brensilver. Source: AudioDharma.

item.title: Guided Meditation on Auditory Thinking
item.description:
What happens when thought is treated not as a private voice, but as something heard within experience? The talk uses auditory thinking as a doorway into mindfulness, showing how deep listening can loosen identification and reveal the gap between a thought and the story built around it. From there it turns toward a quieter practice: allowing unpleasantness, giving up the impulse to control, and meeting experience without making enemies of it.
Location: Online
Chapters: 00:00 Sound and Silence 00:30 Thinking as Sound 01:58 De-reifying Thought 05:20 Narrative and Impermanence 12:34 Pain and Narration 16:06 Open Awareness 22:39 No Enemies 25:07 Who Are You? 29:36 Closing Silence
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/97515/

item.title: Forgiving Conditioning
item.description:
What happens if the first move is not self-improvement, but forgiveness of what has already arisen? The talk traces a quiet shift from body posture and metta into a deep practice of forgiving conditioning, habit, shame, and self-judgment without confusing forgiveness with passivity or condoning harm. It then widens into acceptance, loving-kindness, and a poetic sense of meeting life beyond the usual right and wrong.
Location: Online
Chapters: 00:00 Settling the posture 01:22 Longing for peace 02:43 Forgiving what arises 07:54 Habits and shame 14:52 Putting down the list 20:18 Love as acceptance 24:07 Metta from forgiveness 30:59 Beyond right and wrong
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/97489/

item.title: Guided Meditation: Breath and Sound
item.description:
What happens if meditation is less about forcing calm and more about making room for what is already here? The talk guides a gradual settling into posture, then into breath, and then into sound and silence, repeatedly returning to the idea that practice is simple, humble, and can include imperfection, hope, and fear without fighting them. A brief reference to Sharon Salzberg sharpens the contrast between trying to make practice work and simply doing the practice.
Location: Online
Chapters: 00:00 Settling the posture 02:19 Good enough to begin 06:01 Arriving with mindfulness 09:13 A bigger room 12:48 Breath as enjoyment 18:46 Simply doing practice 23:48 Breathing with hope and fear 28:51 Sound, silence, and trust
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/97464/

item.title: Releasing Judging, Comparing, Fixing: Guided Meditation: Non-Fixing
item.description:
What if meditation is not about making goodness happen, but noticing what is already here? The talk moves from releasing the habit of control and fixing toward a quieter practice of forgiving phenomena, where attention becomes reverential and the mind softens into love. It ends by holding the path as a living balance between dissolving and reification, light and heavy, surrender and power.
Chapters: 00:01 Goodness Already Here 01:45 Settling Into Practice 06:05 Letting Go of Control 07:10 Long Quiet Unfolding 20:16 Space and Stillness 23:13 Forgiveness and Reverence 34:57 Surrender and Love 39:02 Light and Heavy
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/25573

item.title: Releasing Judging, Comparing, Fixing: Landing Here
item.description:
What happens when the habit of judging, comparing, and fixing drops away long enough for experience to settle on its own? The talk moves from simple questions about what is already still and what needs soothing, into a practice of softening the body and mind so attention can land here without measuring against an ideal. It then opens into a sustained reflection on how comparison creates suffering, and how mindfulness can become just being a body breathing, with less and less reference to self-story.
Chapters: 00:00 What Is Already Still 02:24 Softening Toward Arrival 07:09 Mind in the Mind 12:41 Breath Without Fixing 20:27 Renouncing Comparison 23:39 Just a Breathing Body 29:59 Experience and Experiencer
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/25569

item.title: Lightly Guided Meditation
item.description:
What changes when meditation begins with the smallest possible turn toward experience? The talk keeps returning to a simple question: how little energy is needed to start, and what is already here that can be met without strain. From there it moves through soothing tension, widening attention, and resting with the present field instead of forcing the mind to grab an object.
Chapters: 00:00 Starting from here 01:19 The least effort 05:25 Soothing the tension 08:54 Feeding attention well 14:41 Wherever we look 21:11 Not leaning toward 25:09 Closing turn
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/25499

item.title: Guided Meditation: Attention in the body
item.description:
This guided meditation begins by gathering the body, breath, and all the moments of practice into the present, then asks what peace needs to be made with this moment in order to arrive. The attention is widened from the obvious hot spots to the places that are numb or overlooked, with an emphasis on relaxing tension, unifying body and breathing, and letting pleasant, unpleasant, and neutral experience all have a place. As the practice settles, it turns toward the subtle energetics of the body and a quieter, more stable alertness that can soothe the bodily feeling beneath our problems.
Chapters: 00:12 Settling In 01:01 Making Peace 03:47 Body and Breath 05:47 Subtle Sensation 08:10 Relaxing Resistance 10:40 Cohering the Field 14:59 Hope and Fear 19:06 Soothing the Body
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/25455

item.title: Guided Meditation: Effort of Effortlessness (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
What is the wise effort in this moment, and how can attention stay close without turning rigid? The talk moves through the relationship between effort and receptivity, suggesting that practice asks for willingness rather than willfulness, and that attention is continually shaped by what feels salient—hope, fear, suffering, peace. Near the end, the focus turns to retreat ending and the mind in transition: how to meet the rise of agitation, excitement, and planning without losing the thread of practice. An image of “the Buddha whispering in our ear” frames the Dharma less as interruption than as a quiet correction to the way we treat inner experience.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:01 Wise effort 02:18 Activity and receptivity 06:57 Willingness over willfulness 11:42 What matters 18:19 Samadhi and field 23:28 Effortless dependence 32:52 The Buddha's whisper 37:18 Retreat ending 41:28 Taking practice home
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/97005/?access_key=RMLI47PRFc

item.title: Guided Meditation: Equanimity as Love in the Face of Helplessness (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
How do you keep love open when you cannot fix what is happening? The talk begins with suffering, helplessness, and the sense that any honest care will include some pain, then turns toward equanimity as a kind of love that does not insist on changing the world. A story of visiting an elder brother with dementia, the image of the already-broken teacup, and a quiet practice rhythm of compassion and rest all help show how the heart can stay receptive without becoming frantic or numb.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Loss and the question of love 04:09 True love and koans 05:30 Compassion and helplessness 08:58 Holding what cannot be fixed 14:21 Already-broken teacup 18:42 Practice in the body 25:25 Deep time and letting go 31:07 Rest that restores love
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/96946/?access_key=RMLI47PRFc

item.title: Guided Meditation: The Urgency of Feeling and Proliferation (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
How do feeling, urgency, and thought tighten experience into suffering? The talk begins by inviting a broad forgiveness of phenomena—sight and sound, body and mind, self and world—then turns to a close look at how arousal and feeling tone shape grasping, aversion, and the urge to control. It moves gradually into a practice of noticing thoughts, images, and stories as part of the same field, so that urgency can be held in mindfulness rather than followed. By the end, even the sense of "my life" and "my practice" is treated as just another arising of tension to be known and forgiven.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Forgive Phenomena 02:27 Settling Into Anchor 05:29 No Enemy Here 10:23 Urgency in Feeling 17:10 Mindfulness Defangs 22:44 Thinking Orchestrates 29:24 Hearing Thought 34:09 Practice Itself Too 41:41 Closing Encouragement
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/96947/?access_key=RMLI47PRFc

item.title: Guided Meditation: Love in the Face of Suffering (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
What if compassion is not something that fixes suffering, but the only honest way to meet it? The talk moves from the image of wisdom and compassion as “two wings of awakening” into a close look at the Brahma-viharas as lived forms of love, especially compassion as love in the face of suffering. The guided practice then softens self-measurement and self-harshness, using simple aspiration phrases to turn toward pain without pretending it is otherwise.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Two Wings 01:20 Four Kinds of Love 03:48 Suffering And Necessity 05:15 Not Solving Everything 08:39 Preparing To Practice 13:43 Measureless Qualities 15:39 Taking Care Of Suffering 19:52 Heart Phrases 26:11 Care And Forgiveness
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/96943/?access_key=RMLI47PRFc

item.title: Guided Meditation: Letting Go into the Breath (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
Held first by the silence and the sangha, this guided meditation asks what the deep mind needs in order to feel safe enough to let go. The practice moves from scanning the body and softening urgency into feeling the breath more fully, then into a careful noticing of each in-breath and out-breath as something to meet with patience rather than control. From there, the talk widens into walking meditation as a way to train the mind to let go and begin again, balancing the energy of sitting and movement without forcing a special state.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Held by the Sangha 03:04 Reassure the Deep Mind 08:01 Breath in the Whole Body 17:24 Trust the Breath 22:29 Subtlety of Each Breath 31:02 Dharma Happens 41:30 Walking as Practice 48:17 Balance Energy and Steadiness
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/96939/?access_key=RMLI47PRFc

item.title: Guided Meditation: Difficult for the Self to Understand what it can't do
item.description:
The talk centers on the uneasy gap between what we can actually do in meditation and what has to unfold on its own. It moves from simple instructions to find the body, breath, and a relaxed way of pointing attention, then keeps returning to the distinction between attention as an activity and awareness as something we do not manufacture. Along the way, the speaker asks listeners to notice the pull of hope and fear, sort out what is and is not their job, and see how the self can mistake its own effort for the whole Dharma path.
Chapters: 00:11 Arrive in the body 00:50 A relaxed attention 02:04 Hope, fear, and re-dedication 03:24 What is your job 07:54 Staying with the step 15:23 The self and the path 16:43 Awareness larger than story 23:13 Instruction and limitation
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/25380

item.title: Guided Meditation: Awareness is reverential about all phenomena
item.description:
What happens when practice stops sorting experience into pleasant and unpleasant? This guided meditation begins by settling the body and softening the effort to get things right, then turns toward the idea that whatever is present belongs here and is not an accident. The talk gradually opens into a more reverent way of meeting experience: less preference, less strain, and more willingness to listen to what is already here.
Chapters: 00:11 Settling In 01:13 Feeling Good, Feeling Bad 02:56 Releasing Preference 04:17 Just Breathe 07:01 Reverence for Experience 10:04 Steady Attention 12:24 Effort and Ease 14:00 Meeting the Mind 18:59 Listening Within
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/25293

item.title: Lightly Guided Meditation
item.description:
What does it mean to open to this moment without bracing against it? The talk moves gently from obvious tensions like grasping and aversion into subtler habits of control, then into a practice of trusting surrender and noticing the lines we draw between what should happen and what is happening. It keeps returning to a sober, unclenched attention that doesn’t try to protect experience from its own impermanence.
Chapters: 00:12 Opening to this moment 01:19 Putting down control 05:39 Breath and bracing 07:51 Urgency without strain 13:50 Awake, not deliberate 17:48 Awareness and vulnerability 23:42 What can be harmed
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/25234

item.title: Guided Meditation: Dedicating our practice
item.description:
What does it mean to arrive here, body and mind, without bracing against experience? This guided meditation begins with settling and “putting it down,” then turns toward dedicating the practice—not as pressure, but as a quiet recognition that practice matters because life is finite and not a dress rehearsal. As the talk unfolds, love is invited to gather in the background while the breath, the path, and the wish for peace are honored. The practice movement is gentle but serious: softening defensiveness, letting love stand beside what cannot be controlled, and sensing how the world looks different when love meets finitude.
Chapters: 00:30 Arriving Here 02:01 Putting It Down 03:20 Dedicating Practice 10:29 Love and Finitude 12:16 Quiet Repetition 19:44 Seeing Differently
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/25130

item.title: Guided Meditation: safe to relax
item.description:
How much effort does this moment actually call for, and can that effort come from ease rather than strain? The talk repeatedly returns to the simple instruction to breathe in a way that tells the deep mind it is okay to relax, even amid fragility, imperfection, and the tenuousness of things. A brief Rumi quotation opens into a Buddhist reflection on stepping out of self-view, loosening identification, and letting go of the need to hold everything as "I" and "mine."
Chapters: 00:12 Calibrating Effort 01:53 Ease in Practice 04:34 Safety in the Breath 08:58 Rumi and Rest 13:19 Making No Objects 17:30 Trusting Release
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/25072

item.title: Guided Meditation: Refuge
item.description:
This guided meditation keeps returning to a simple instruction: take refuge, and let the heart soften. The practice moves from naming refuge in Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha to calling in love, gratitude, or reverence, then settling attention in the body, the breath, and the place where body meets space. Later, the talk widens the frame, suggesting that a life needs a vantage point bigger than the life itself and that we can rest in the stream of Dharma, wisdom, and love.
Chapters: 00:12 Entering Refuge 01:56 Love and Reverence 03:46 Settling the Body 06:10 Return to Refuge 09:57 Dharma Across Time 15:05 Less Identified 22:22 Closing Insight
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/25028

The practice moves from naming refuge in Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha to calling in love, gratitude, or reverence, then settling attention in the body, the breath, and the place where body meets space. Later, the talk widens the frame, suggesting that a life needs a vantage point bigger than the life itself and that we can rest in the stream of Dharma, wisdom, and love.
Chapters: 00:12 Entering Refuge 01:56 Love and Reverence 03:46 Settling the Body 06:10 Return to Refuge 09:57 Dharma Across Time 15:05 Less Identified 22:22 Closing Insight
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/25028

item.title: Guided Meditation: non-referentiality
item.description:
The talk moves from a simple instruction—find something that feels good—into a subtler practice of resting with barely noticeable pleasantness without making it into an object. From there it loosens the usual sense of a meditator looking from a separate vantage point, inviting a shift out of representation and referential thinking into open, unpartitioned space. Along the way it returns again and again to the body’s ripples of sensation, and to a gentler relationship with time, pain, and the mind’s habit of keeping score.
Chapters: 00:12 Settling with the pleasant 01:17 A soft smile 02:38 Dropping the vantage point 04:35 Renounce orientation 06:19 Fear and love 08:31 Relaxing more deeply 17:09 Not bracing against pain 21:06 The grammar of self 25:49 Trees and arrival
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/24993

item.title: Guided Meditation: The Safety of Letting Go
item.description:
What happens when thinking itself is treated as a safety behavior? The guided meditation begins with posture and breath, then moves into deep listening: noticing body sensations, hearing the activity of thought without obeying it, and offering the mind some instant forgiveness. From there, the practice widens into a gentle permission for feeling, less need to narrate experience, and a gradual relinquishing of control into the reality of this moment.
Chapters: 00:09 Settling In 01:29 Unifying the Body 04:23 Deep Listening 08:45 Thinking as Protection 12:33 Permission for Feeling 15:20 This Moment 20:38 Relinquishing Control
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/24838

item.title: Guided Meditation: Resting in Goodness
item.description:
Beginning with a comic brush against existential dread, the talk moves from bleak, relatable quotes into a simple question: what happens if, instead of tightening around distress, we settle and let goodness in? From there it becomes a guided meditation on breath, body, and appreciation, including the imperfect body and the possibility of resting with ordinary life as it is. Later, the talk returns to the tenderness of being here at all, contrasting a harsh inner disciplinarian with a quieter sense that enjoyment may be the deepest way of life.
Chapters: 00:19 Comic Prelude 02:21 Finding Posture 05:19 Settling in Goodness 09:26 Resting With Breath 13:06 Being Here 17:17 Enjoyment Deeply 19:47 Letting Go 23:27 Fear and Uncertainty
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/24760

item.title: Guided Meditation: Sitting as if already dead
item.description:
The talk moves from simple settling—relaxing the body, softening the heart, and not bartering with the future—into a more radical instruction: sit as if already dead, awake, and no longer negotiating with fear. From that imagined release, the speaker explores how self-identity and hatred loosen, leaving more room to rest, love, and take cues from loving-kindness rather than aversion. Near the end, the emphasis tightens into a plain reminder to slow down and be still, with the suggestion that hatred is never the last word.
Chapters: 00:12 Settling and relaxing 01:00 Softening the heart 02:40 Space from samsara 05:32 Not bartering with future 08:20 Already dead, awake 12:57 Love without fear 19:34 Mettā made vast 25:25 Hatred is not final
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/24705

item.title: Guided Practice: Compassion and Equanimity (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
What do you do when love cannot fix what is in front of you? This guided practice begins by framing the Dharma as a living balance between wholesome forces, then turns to compassion and equanimity as love in the face of suffering and love in the face of helplessness. The talk moves back and forth between prayerful aspiration, a stiller body, and a sober recognition that the heart must learn both to act and to rest when it has reached the end of its power.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Middle path, living balance 01:45 Compassion and equanimity 06:04 Settling into practice 12:02 The trembling heart 13:54 Love and mortality 21:03 Return to compassion
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/94977/

item.title: Guided Meditation: What Does it Take to Land Here? (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
What does it take to actually land in this moment, without pretending the fear or agitation isn’t here? The talk begins with a simple ritual of turning in phones, then moves into a guided sitting practice that keeps returning to breath, body, and the question of what needs to be known or blessed with awareness in order to let go safely. Later, it opens into walking meditation instructions, emphasizing a plain rhythm of stillness and movement, and a way of sensing the body directly rather than living only in thought.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Opening Ceremony 02:20 Settling Into Silence 08:31 Letting Go Safely 15:30 Honest Practice 23:44 Breath and Fear 34:09 Deepening Into Rest 49:32 Walking Meditation 57:33 Where to Walk
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/94973/

item.title: Guided Metta meditation
item.description:
How do you practice metta without pretending to feel what is not there? The talk moves from a clear argument that loving-kindness must honor the truth of the moment into a guided practice that uses phrases, images, and simple attention to settle into the body and let awareness soften around what is present. It returns again and again to the idea that metta can be cultivation, concentration, or purification: sometimes a single word is enough, sometimes grief rises, and sometimes the practice is just letting time be an ally. In the later Q&A, the talk turns toward the relationship between love and grief, and toward the four Brahma-viharas as different kinds of love that fit different situations.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening frame 02:07 Finding the right love 05:57 Measureless awareness 12:00 Guided metta practice 40:31 Practice in the world 50:20 Constriction and worthiness 54:42 Love and grief 01:00:54 Closing instructions
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/24617

item.title: Guided Meditation: entering the dharma realm
item.description:
How do we deliberately step into a field shaped by kindness, clarity, and letting go, so that experience itself is less dominated by suffering? The talk moves from refuge as a way of meeting the world to the idea of entering a “Dharma realm” where body, thought, and even grief are interpreted differently. It then turns toward the second arrow: Dharma cannot erase the first suffering, but it can meet the added pain with care, love, and a quieter sense of belonging.
Chapters: 00:11 Taking Refuge 01:07 Why Refuge Matters 03:52 Entering the Dharma Field 07:08 Second Arrow 08:52 Goodwill and Renunciation 11:24 Subtle Change 17:11 Breath and Love 22:58 What Love Asks
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/24554

item.title: Lightly Guided Meditation
item.description:
The talk begins by asking what happens when control is set down and the body is allowed to relax. From there, it moves into a quiet instruction to stop rehearsing identity, to let pleasant be pleasant and unpleasant be unpleasant without adding more story. The middle of the meditation leans on equanimity as the condition for non-proliferation: unpleasantness can be felt as just the end of a sentence, with nothing more to say or do. It closes with a tender image of the moment as something that meets pain only where healing is needed, inviting the whole body-mind to soften and trust that whatever is discovered can deepen the case for love.
Chapters: 00:12 Settling the body 01:00 Putting down identity 02:02 Letting experience be 07:10 Equanimity and non-proliferation 09:37 Very alive to this moment 11:03 Softening into love 12:39 Closing bell meditation
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/24450

item.title: Guided Meditation: Sound and Silence
item.description:
What changes when listening stops being a task and becomes a way of letting sound, thought, and silence meet without grasping? The talk moves gently from simple instruction into a widening field of attention: external sounds, internal sounds, the sense of self, and the moments when silence seems to get louder. It returns again and again to not picking things up, so they can become less sticky and more easily left alone.
Chapters: 01:00 Entering Listening 02:30 Hunger and Sound 04:00 Listening in All Directions 06:15 Self With No Job 07:30 No Inside or Outside 12:20 Equanimity and Nonclinging 15:37 Bowing to Preoccupations 22:33 Edge of Silence
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/24399

item.title: Guided Meditation: Ease Makes Letting Go Safer
item.description:
The practice begins with finding a breath that carries a signal of ease into the body, then letting that ease coexist with whatever agitation is still present. Rather than trying to force discomfort away, the talk keeps returning to the places that are already soothed, asking attention to rest there and let awareness do its quieter work. From that steadier vantage, the future feels less like a problem to solve and more like something met by taking good care of what is here now. The meditation closes by settling into a subtle warmth, perhaps even the smallest smile, and recognizing that simply abiding is enough.
Chapters: 00:12 Settling the breath 01:11 Rest with ease 02:33 Care for the present 04:50 Awareness changes carrying 08:42 Wide world, simple attention 14:22 Letting go feels safer 20:29 Warmth and abiding
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/24241

item.title: Guided Meditation: Activity and Receptivity
item.description:
The talk turns on a simple question: when do we need effort, and when do we need receptivity? Using the image of entering water, the speaker explores how letting go often has to be gradual, as if the body itself needs consent to release its grip, and how faith and safety shape that movement. From there, the practice becomes a careful balancing of directing attention and surrendering into awareness, noticing how non-distraction can bring stability when the moment calls for it. The talk closes by returning to that balance between activity and receptivity, with some compassion for the intensity of being human.
Chapters: 00:00 Entering Gradually 01:55 Body Consent 04:16 Illusions of Control 06:16 Doing and Surrender 10:49 Balancing the Mind 13:13 Against Hierarchy 17:25 Non-Distraction 19:23 Compassionate Balance
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/24177

item.title: Guided Meditation: Bright, Steady, Open
item.description:
The talk begins with a simple shift in perspective: life is not out in front of us, but all around and through us. From there, the practice moves toward knowing with brightness, steadiness, and equanimity, without collapsing everything back into “me,” and toward a kind of shared permission to practice together. It also lingers with the difference between bowing to conditioning and fully giving in to it, ending with a spacious, humbling question about what we are from the point of view of the universe.
Chapters: 00:00 Settling Into Context 00:43 Self and Suffering 02:08 Brightness and Stability 05:21 Permission to Practice 07:27 Time as Ally 11:47 Humility and Effort 17:09 Opening the Frame 23:31 Not Much, Not Nothing
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/24140

item.title: Guided Meditation: Relax and release
item.description:
How deeply can the mind let go of the future, the need to control, and the habit of turning practice into another project? This guided meditation begins with relaxing body and mind, then repeatedly returns attention to the present moment as a way to nurture stability, clarity, and alertness without getting captured by clinging. It moves through renunciation, refuge, and a quieter willingness to stop insisting that experience must mean something other than itself.
Chapters: 00:01 Settling In 01:00 Present Moment 03:30 Steady Attention 05:49 Not Clinging 08:48 Renouncing Effort 10:05 What We Carry 13:18 Taking Refuge 18:23 No Need to Mean 20:11 Closing Rest
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/24106

item.title: Guided Meditation: Allowing (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
How do you stop fighting what is already here? The talk keeps returning to a simple, repeated permission: forgive all phenomena, do not fight your fight, and let the present moment be met without adding another layer of resistance. As the meditation unfolds, the instruction widens from softening the body and letting awareness receive thoughts, sounds, and sensations, to a final release of self-judgment: nothing you do in these minutes is wrong. Near the end, the talk briefly turns to retreat-community care and boundaries, using a harmful note as a reminder that practice also depends on how carefully people move around one another.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Opening forgiveness 01:00 Not fighting resistance 10:18 Softening into Dharma 17:26 Settling the spasms 21:46 No autobiography needed 26:12 Yielding to awareness 31:20 Nothing is wrong 39:14 Closing announcements
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/93307/

item.title: Guided Meditation: Relax a Little Further (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
If you still think you know who you are, the talk keeps returning to one instruction: relax a little further. It moves from loosening the grip on progress, control, and self-judgment into a practice of gentle attention, receptivity, and surrender—until suffering can be met without making it into identity. Later, the guidance widens into steadier, more open attention, a softer relationship to pain, and the sense that refuge is not confined to one special state or place.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Relax further 05:29 Doing and surrender 09:57 Attitude and renunciation 16:12 Attention opens 18:17 Care for pain 24:28 Receiving what is here 30:44 Love without preference 38:19 Refuge everywhere 44:08 Retreat notes and close
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/93303/

item.title: Guided Meditation: Relaxing all clinging
item.description:
Relaxation here is treated as more than softening the body: it becomes a gradual surrender of the agenda to control, the familiar stories, and the narrow identifications that keep the mind tense. The guided practice moves toward noticing fatigue, letting the heart and mind release, and resting in silence with more kindness for conditioning and less need to hold onto self. By the end, the talk points to a trust that seeds of goodness are already present, and that awareness can feel less and less like self.
Chapters: 00:00 Settling into surrender 00:50 Releasing the familiar 04:37 Fatigue with old patterns 09:06 Longing to rest 10:58 Stepping out of identification 14:40 Forgiveness and kindness 17:22 Trusting goodness 22:10 Quiet ending
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/24004

item.title: Guided Meditation: How distant is freedom?
item.description:
How far away is peace, and what if the feeling of distance is partly made by the mind itself? This guided meditation turns toward the breath with a softer kind of attention, asking when focus is too tight, when it can relax, and how effort can quietly become control. The practice keeps returning to the body, to the sense of lineage and support, and to a simple trust that the moment does not have to be managed in order for mindfulness to be present.
Chapters: 00:12 How Far Is Peace 01:00 All Practice Is Here 03:54 Taking Our Cues 05:54 Softening Control 08:36 Boundaries Without Grasping 11:24 Mindfulness and Ungovernability 13:07 Trusting the Moment 22:09 Resting in Silence
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/23939
item.itunes:author: Matthew Brensilver
item.itunes:summary:
How far away is peace, and what if the feeling of distance is partly made by the mind itself? This guided meditation turns toward the breath with a softer kind of attention, asking when focus is too tight, when it can relax, and how effort can quietly become control. The practice keeps returning to the body, to the sense of lineage and support, and to a simple trust that the moment does not have to be managed in order for mindfulness to be present.
Chapters: 00:12 How Far Is Peace 01:00 All Practice Is Here 03:54 Taking Our Cues 05:54 Softening Control 08:36 Boundaries Without Grasping 11:24 Mindfulness and Ungovernability 13:07 Trusting the Moment 22:09 Resting in Silence
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/23939

item.title: Guided Meditation: Internal and External Sound and Silence
item.description:
What changes when listening stops favoring the mind’s inner narration over a bird, a plane, or the quiet around them? The talk guides attention through external sound, internal sound, and the silence between and behind them, with repeated invitations to soften, notice the body’s porous edge with space, and meet experience without preference. From there it turns toward how meaning-making often grows out of fear, and how simple listening can become a form of prayer and non-violence.
Location: Big Bear Retreat Center
Chapters: 00:00 Entering Silence 01:24 Outer and Inner Sound 03:28 No Preference 07:31 Receptive Listening 12:09 Body and Space 15:10 Meaning and Fear 16:57 Dharma as Blessing 19:32 Listening in Prayer 27:04 Nonviolence in Practice
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/93052/

Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/93052/

item.title: Guided Meditation: Forgiving Body, Mind and World
item.description:
What happens when practice is no longer aimed at producing calm, but at meeting pain, friction, and even collapse without fighting? The talk moves from noticing the difference between what the mind craves and what it needs, into a gentler stance of making peace with the body, the mind, and the human condition. It keeps returning to forgiveness as a kind of truce: with grasping, aversion, sorrow, and the impulse to control experience. By the end, the invitation is simple and quiet—rest in the awareness that remains when there is nothing left to do.
Location: Big Bear Retreat Center
Chapters: 00:00 Settling into need 05:45 Making peace 08:09 Forgive body and mind 11:23 Global softening 16:29 Nothing left to do 22:27 Breath and non-friction 31:27 Everything tastes like Dharma 38:15 Rest in awareness
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/93050/

item.title: Guided Meditation: Urgency of Feeling
item.description:
This guided meditation begins with fuller breaths and a slow settling into the body, then turns toward the subtle pressure behind restlessness: the wish to fix, achieve, heal, or make feeling go away. The practice keeps returning to sensation—especially the breath, the face, jaw, shoulders, belly, and spine—while noticing how surges of feeling can create urgency without needing to be obeyed. Again and again, the talk points to a quiet alternative to control: relax where you can, absorb the impact of feeling, and let the breath become safe enough to return to.
Location: Big Bear Retreat Center
Chapters: 00:00 Settling the body 02:52 Relaxation cues 06:05 Hidden agendas 10:25 Feeling as urgency 14:23 False alarms 19:50 Breath and equanimity 28:39 Relaxing the threat
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/93048/

item.title: Guided Meditation: Relaxing and Letting Feeling be Feeling
item.description:
When unpleasantness arises, can it simply be known as unpleasantness without needing to be fixed, explained, or turned into a story about who you are? The talk keeps returning to that question through a quiet guided practice of relaxing the jaw, belly, and mind; narrowing attention to the breath; and then widening it again when pain, fear, or restlessness need some room. Rather than asking you to push experience away, it moves toward a radical inclusion in which feeling can stay feeling, and the body can soften without pretending anything is absent.
Location: Big Bear Retreat Center
Chapters: 00:01 Know Feeling Simply 01:22 Relax the Urgency 05:24 Soften the Body 08:53 Feel the Breath 14:16 Uncontrived Breathing 18:06 Subtle In-Breath and Out 21:25 Widen the Aperture 28:35 Radical Inclusion 33:50 Necessity of Love
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/93046/

item.title: Guided Meditation: moving towards compassion
item.description:
How can compassion be grown without denying the fact of suffering? The talk begins with a brief Issa poem about dukkha and then settles into guided practice: first softening the body and gathering the mind, then meeting heaviness, sorrow, and helplessness with a thoroughly undefended kindness. From there it turns toward the way love can be mixed with pain, supported by mindfulness, stillness, and the quiet wish that suffering lessen.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening and Issa poem 01:47 Settling the body 06:11 Awake to experience 10:42 Meeting pain gently 13:53 Love meets suffering 16:50 Turning toward love 20:55 Body as antenna 23:32 Planting non-violence 25:47 Closing reflection
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/23818

item.title: Guided Meditation: don't wait to let go
item.description:
The meditation begins with posture, breath, and a felt yielding to gravity: finding both suppleness and strength, then giving the rest of body and mind to the earth. From there, the talk turns toward impermanence, suggesting that when we stop imagining ourselves as permanent, even one breath can feel enough; that same recognition becomes the basis for the refrain, “don’t wait to let go.” The practice movement is simple and direct—soothing the body, loosening grasping, and noticing how surrender changes the way everything appears. Near the end, the talk widens into loving-kindness and a brief personal reflection on grief and a friend’s death.
Chapters: 00:00 Settling the body 02:41 Yielding to gravity 06:23 Finitude and enough 12:16 Do not wait 13:08 Resting after surrender 22:25 Practice loving 23:44 More space around pain 25:59 Remembering Kathleen
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/23721

item.title: Guided Meditation: Abiding in Care
item.description:
What changes when kindness is not used as a strategy, but as a way of abiding? The talk moves through a guided settling into posture, softening tension and agitation, and letting love become an ambience of the mind rather than something performed or forced. From there, it opens into the tenderness of being human: the body, breath, grief, joy, and the way compassion can ease self-absorption without denying pain.
Chapters: 00:27 Settling In 01:43 Softening Tension 03:44 The Heart's Realm 06:17 No Need to Perform 08:47 Finding a Support 11:57 Love and Spaciousness 16:51 Four Forms of Love 19:37 Breath and Presence 28:01 Grief and Poignancy
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/23687

item.title: Guided Meditation: Renunciation and Dana
item.description:
What if renunciation is not a harsh refusal, but a softening of the grip that keeps greed, aversion, and selfing in place? The talk moves from a simple meditation instruction—lay down greed and distress, as the Satipatthana says—into a gentler understanding of letting go as unclenching, relaxing, and releasing the anxious project of control. From there, it opens into generosity: when ownership loosens, life feels less like something to manage and more like gift, with breath, body, and daily life becoming forms of dāna.
Chapters: 00:11 Opening and theme 01:53 What to put down 04:20 Unclenching the fist 06:45 Greed and distress 09:39 Letting control soften 15:16 Ownership and gratitude 18:25 Breath as dāna 22:57 Life becomes gift
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/23673

item.title: Guided Meditation: Everything is Passing
item.description:
What changes when practice is seen not as a stable state to reach, but as something always moving? The talk begins with a gentle settling into breath and stillness, then widens into a meditation on reverence, care, lovingkindness, and forgiveness for our conditioning. From there it turns to impermanence: not just that things pass, but that everything is always passing, including the sense of self that seems to be watching it all.
Chapters: 00:00 Arriving and settling 02:00 Calling in practice 04:30 Reverence and devotion 07:25 Loving-kindness and care 10:23 Understanding wandering 14:28 Forgiving conditioning 18:17 The flow of anicca 25:06 Melting into breathing
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/23662

item.title: Guided Meditation: Emphasizing Tranquility
item.description:
What happens if tranquility is less something we force than something we let settle? The talk keeps returning to that motion of easing off willfulness, yielding to silence, and noticing the restful texture already present in body and mind. It also names practical adjustments for tired or bright states, including how to work with agitation without demanding its absence. Near the end, the emphasis deepens into simplicity: as hunger in the mind subsides, less and less is needed, until just being alive can feel like enough.
Chapters: 00:00 Settling In 00:30 Ceasing to Strive 01:29 Yielding to Silence 02:49 Finding the Quiet Beneath 05:30 Brightness Or Rest 07:26 Listening to Tranquility 10:51 Signs of Calm 15:01 Putting Down Burdens 18:14 Enoughness and Closure
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/23669

item.title: Guided Meditation Presence is not a Transaction
item.description:
Start by settling the body well enough for a kind of truce with its imperfection, then let the breath and the closed-eye stillness signal that this moment is good enough to begin letting go. The talk keeps returning to a simple but demanding move: notice how the mind keeps propping up stories, fears, and control, and see the relief in setting some of that down. Midway through, the teaching sharpens into receiving experience in a non-transactional way—without trying to extract something from the moment—and trusting the moment as a teacher or medicine. It closes by widening the frame: meditation is not placed beside life, but life itself is held within the Dharma.
Chapters: 00:34 Settling the Body 02:37 Breath as Permission 04:56 Stories Held Loosely 06:23 Worry and Safety 08:35 Enduring the Practice 14:51 Receiving Without Taking 19:32 Life Within Dharma
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/23655

item.title: Guided Meditation: Clinging is complicated, letting go simplifies
item.description:
Clinging is shown as tangled and effortful, while letting go is presented as simple, even if not always easy. The talk moves gently through settling the body, feeling the impact of hope, fear, and memory without finishing their stories, and resting attention in breathing. At one point, the breathing is framed as “whispers from the Buddha,” inviting a soft release into the present moment. The guidance keeps returning to a quiet balance: meet experience with warmth, and lay down just enough complexity to love and serve more deeply.
Chapters: 00:12 Opening and posture 02:05 Taking up practice slowly 04:12 Laying down complexity 07:50 Hope, fear, and the body 11:16 Breath as release 16:49 Clinging and stickiness 21:26 Gentle warmth
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/23706

item.title: Guided meditation in Style of Shinzen Young: Mental Images, Auditory Thinking and Emotion in the Body
item.description:
How does a moment become suffering when sight, sound, feeling, and inner talk quietly harden into “me and my problems”? This guided meditation starts with settling the body, then moves through internal visual impressions, auditory thinking, and emotion in the body, showing how each sense gate can be noticed directly rather than followed. The practice keeps returning to a simple movement: hear thought as thought, feel feeling as feeling, and let experience unfold without taking it as a contract with the moment.
Chapters: 00:01 Settling the body 02:27 Mindfulness and posture 05:31 Seeing mental images 11:58 Hearing inner thought 17:33 Thought and orientation 24:41 Feeling in the body 31:58 The three sense gates 37:34 Tracking the triad 42:26 How suffering forms
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/23625

item.title: Lightly Guided Meditation
item.description:
What happens when meditation starts not with effort, but with a felt sense of goodness? The talk moves from goodwill and the “longing for less suffering” into a gentle practice of settling the body, narrowing attention without hostility, and resting with breathing, sound, and body as the foreground while everything else is left in the background. It then opens into a broader reflection on how trust in goodness can make the future feel less intimidating and make rest feel possible, even when care and responsibility have been running hard. Near the end, the talk turns to the need for cycles of love and rest, suggesting that always staying on duty can erode the very capacity one wants to preserve.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening goodwill 00:57 Settling posture 02:33 Goodness as practice 04:35 Trust and security 06:28 Clear attention 07:22 Resting now 11:08 Releasing memory 17:24 Care and rest
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/23614

item.title: Guided Meditation: If you're looking, you're lost
item.description:
The talk begins by turning meditation away from effortful searching: rather than looking for a better state, the instruction is to find what genuinely inspires longing for the Dharma and then notice what serves in this moment. From there, the emphasis shifts to a very direct practice of inclusion—letting pleasure and pain be met more equally, and seeing how willfulness and doingness can quietly block the path. The closing movement is toward less and less strain: attention no longer needs to be propped up so aggressively, and awareness is described as continuing even when that effort is dropped.
Chapters: 00:12 Settling In 01:24 Finding Inspiration 02:14 Longing, Not Craving 04:29 What Serves 07:20 Don't Go Looking 14:07 Radical Inclusion 17:17 Letting Go of Willfulness 23:58 Awareness Needs No Job
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/23490

item.title: Guided Meditation: Letting go of limitation
item.description:
The talk begins by settling the body and then gently asks what it would mean to put down ideas of limitation. From there, it explores how clinging makes the mind self-absorbed and narrow, and how a little less self-protection can open into safety, breath, and a sense of vastness. Later, the speaker turns to equanimity: not preferring the expansive over the contractive, and not trying to manufacture openness, but letting conditions soften on their own.
Chapters: 00:12 Grounding and Settling 02:42 Putting Down Limitation 05:31 Clinging and Vastness 09:39 Safety in the Body 15:32 No Preference 16:12 Nothing to Manufacture 21:23 Samsara and Weariness 26:14 Closing Blessing
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/23459

item.title: Guided Meditation: Grounding and Compassion
item.description:
Begin by listening for silence, even when there is noise around you, and let thinking be received as just another kind of sound. The practice then settles into a quieter place, using an image of nectar sinking to the bottom of a glass to point to the body-mind unwinding into steadiness. From there, the talk turns toward compassion: first feeling the reality of suffering, then bringing someone to mind and gradually meeting their pain with warmth rather than defensiveness.
Chapters: 00:12 Entering Silence 01:29 Thoughts as Sound 04:44 Settling Like Nectar 10:03 Suffering and Gratitude 13:45 Bringing Someone to Mind 16:43 Taking It In 18:51 Love in Response 20:50 Softening Into Compassion
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/23421

item.title: Guided Meditation: Forgive all phenomena
item.description:
The practice starts by softening into posture rather than forcing anything, then turns toward the fatigue of always reaching for the next thing. The talk names samsara and craving as a repetitive habit of pursuit, even when it shows up as spiritual progress, and keeps returning to the simplicity of just one breath. In the guided section, the instruction becomes direct: forgive all phenomena, including pleasantness and pain, and trust the settling that follows.
Chapters: 00:12 Opening Into Practice 00:57 Settling the Body 04:08 Bored With Samsara 06:54 The Pull of Next 08:49 Just This Breath 12:49 A Few Conscious Breaths 19:38 Forgive All Phenomena 21:24 Resting In Ease
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/23370

item.title: Guided Meditation: Meditating on June 19
item.description:
The talk begins with the question of where an anchor can rest—sometimes in the breath, sometimes in the body, sometimes as simple awareness itself. From there it moves into how the mind leaks into each moment and how practice can meet the surges, urgencies, and emotional currents of being human without adding more struggle. A Juneteenth reflection opens into a wider point: staying close to love by feeling the intensity of human life, the longing for peace, and a kind of radical nonviolence toward all phenomena, including the self. The meditation then settles into the image of peace at every sense door—body, eye, ear, and mind—as a quiet love at the gate of each experience.
Chapters: 02:30 Finding the Anchor 04:42 Staying with Awareness 07:47 Juneteenth and Freedom 12:13 Peace at the Senses 15:53 Stopping the Fight 17:19 Settling in Silence
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/23239

item.title: Guided Meditation: Following Interest
item.description:
The talk begins with a quiet image of settling—like nectar sinking to the bottom of a glass—and uses it to explore what happens when meditation stops straining to hold everything in place. From there, it moves through the relief of not forcing attention, the fear that progress depends on control, and the invitation to trust that doing nothing can still be enough. It then turns toward a different relationship with awareness: following the thread of interest, letting curiosity animate practice, and offering attention as receptivity rather than as something used to get a result.
Chapters: 00:12 Settling Like Nectar 01:01 Letting Things Surface 04:34 Trusting Non-Doing 08:41 Following Interest 09:29 Curiosity as Practice 15:15 Receptive Attention 17:04 Counting and Closing
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/23189

item.title: Guided Meditation: Love and Dissolving Boundaries
item.description:
The talk begins by noticing how quickly life’s goodness becomes invisible, and how the mind shifts into constant fixing and optimization. From there, the practice turns toward appreciation for this body, this breath, and generosity, letting gratitude soften the tendency to see only problems. Renunciation is framed not as withdrawal, but as a kind of love: a quiet openness where urgency dissolves and nothing has to stick. The guided sequence then repeatedly invites softness, easing wherever the world seems to need you until only softness remains.
Chapters: 00:00 Appreciating good fortune 02:38 Beginning with gratitude 04:17 Silent thanking 06:05 Renunciation as love 08:23 Quiet openheartedness 11:42 Softening the world 14:52 Conditions well enough
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://irc.audiodharma.org/talks/23107

item.title: Guided Meditation: Embodiment and Allowing
item.description:
What does it mean to become available to this moment, rather than trying to force the mind into place? This guided meditation moves gently through relaxation, embodiment, and the feel of breathing in the belly and bones, while also noticing how relaxation can reveal aversion, vulnerability, and the limits of trying to manage experience with words. The emphasis stays on softening, opening awareness, and treating each moment as a fresh beginning, even when practice feels impossible. By the end, the talk points toward a freedom that does not announce itself, but is met by loving what arises without grasping.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening Intention 04:58 Willingness Over Willfulness 06:48 Relaxing into Newness 10:12 Body as Refuge 13:37 Breath into the Bones 16:01 Coping and Openness 17:55 Softening the Impossible 28:09 Freedom Without a Sign
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://irc.audiodharma.org/talks/23092

item.title: Guided Meditation: Fresh Moment
item.description:
This guided meditation starts by noticing how natural it is to expect the next moment to be more of the same, and then gently loosens that habit. The practice moves through relaxing what can be relaxed, settling into the body and breath, and meeting impermanence without being startled by it. Later, the speaker invites following whatever genuine interest arises, and then turns toward the freshness of not quite knowing, putting down expectations and sensing the quiet. The talk closes by asking what it means to cease reiterating the self and to simply breathe, feel the body, and rest in the moment.
Chapters: 00:12 Arriving and settling 00:32 Not startled by change 03:08 Breath as soothing support 06:26 Allowing the present moment 12:12 Following genuine interest 17:17 The aliveness of not knowing 21:06 Releasing expectation 21:46 Quiet and selfing
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/23004

item.title: Guided Meditation: dropping secret agendas (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
What happens when meditation is driven by secret agendas, grasping, or the wish to make experience go a certain way? This guided practice keeps returning to a simple counter-movement: relax, stop looking for the Dharma, and let attention soften into breathing, body, and sound. The talk also turns to walking practice, where the work is to begin again and again, learning to stay calm, alert, and unforced while moving.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:01 Settle and relax 02:28 Hidden agendas 05:57 Wanting and attention 10:48 Let the self loosen 18:12 Surrendering words 19:22 Practice in the hall 39:47 First and second arrows 42:21 Walking as training
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/90259/

item.title: Guided Metta Meditation - Metta for All Beings (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
What if metta is not mainly a feeling, but a way of stepping out of self-judgment altogether? The talk begins by framing the Brahma-viharas as four forms of love, then moves through how loving-kindness can cultivate the heart, gather attention, and purify by bringing up what has not yet been loved. A long guided practice follows, starting with the body and breath, softening self-view, and widening toward phrases for oneself and then for all beings, even while the impossibility of ending suffering remains in view.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Four kinds of love 01:57 Love changes self-view 05:08 Measureless and quiet 06:23 Cultivation and purification 11:48 Settling into practice 22:44 Phrases in the present 26:46 Resting in kindness 31:13 Love in hard places 33:15 All beings at ease
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/90257/

item.title: Guided Meditation: Non-referentiality
item.description:
The meditation starts by loosening the ordinary ways we orient ourselves: body, preference, craving, aversion, and the habit of continually locating who and where we are. From there, the talk repeatedly returns to the question of what it might feel like to be present without those reference points, using the phrase “without referentiality” as a refrain. The practice moves through settling, softening, and letting experience arise and dissolve without needing to pick anything up or define oneself by it, ending with a Rumi line about living in the “nowhere” you come from.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening and arrival 02:20 Brightness and body 05:33 Held by earth 07:09 Softening more 08:39 Reference points 10:59 Without referentiality 21:41 Endings and dissolution 22:52 Nowhere you come from
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/22904

item.title: Guided Medtiation: Checking Hindrances, Checking Defilements
item.description:
The practice starts with a simple body landing, then moves into a careful scan of the five hindrances: restlessness, sloth, desire, aversion, and doubt. Rather than treating them as failures, the talk frames them as ordinary signals in the nervous system and invites a quiet “pulse check” of whatever is present. From there, the guidance keeps returning to how attention can get mixed with wanting, and how to soften into an unmixed awareness that bows rather than grasps. The talk closes by pointing toward surrender, with less effort to manufacture goodness and more willingness to let it be found.
Chapters: 00:12 Settling In 02:20 Scanning Hindrances 05:19 Bearing Unpleasantness 09:40 Choosing an Anchor 12:05 Unmixed Attention 17:33 Letting Go Of Control 21:47 Putting Down Yardsticks
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/22860

item.title: Guided Meditation: Softening the Self
item.description:
What happens if you stop trying to manage the mind and instead relax around it? This guided meditation begins with posture, then moves through softening, widening, and resting attention on the mind’s brightness without forcing it. The talk returns again and again to the felt knot of selfing, asking how much loosens when clinging, striving, and willfulness are allowed to unwind.
Chapters: 00:12 Arrive and Settle 01:00 Relax into Brightness 02:40 Held by Love 05:17 Clinging and Letting Go 11:43 The Knot of Self 15:42 Softening and Widening 21:21 Impermanence and Porosity 28:37 Closing Reflection
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/22800

item.title: Guided Meditation: Arousal and Valence Signifying Nothing
item.description:
Begin with the simple instruction to rest on something settled, then let attention notice how activation and intensity can coexist with a place in the field that is soothed and soothing. The meditation keeps returning to bodily arousal and valence—pleasant, unpleasant, and in-between—showing how craving runs on feeling and how feeling can be given space without being turned immediately into story. Over and over, the practice is to decouple sensation from the automatic loops of thought, let feeling signify nothing, and come back to the breathing until the ordinary reference points begin to loosen.
Location: Big Bear Retreat Center
Chapters: 00:00 Settling and Soothe 01:30 Pleasant and Unpleasant 04:45 Why Feeling Feels Safe 07:51 Tracking Arousal 10:14 Giving Feeling Space 13:14 Worth and Unconscious Feeling 17:19 Tranquility Pulls In 23:06 Decoupling Thought Loops 31:15 Resting in Breath
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/91356/

item.title: Guided Meditation: Surrendering the Territory of Me
item.description:
The talk begins by narrowing attention to the faintest sound, then to darkness behind closed eyes, and then to the settled weight of the body. From there it turns toward the felt boundary of “me” — the urge to patrol, defend, and sort experience into mine and not mine — and invites a softer, more intimate relation to that contraction. The practice movement is toward laying down the fight, relaxing the territorial habit, and noticing that self and world are woven together in the same field of phenomena. By the end, the emphasis is on deep non-preference: whatever arises, even pain or densest selfing, can be met as another moment of offering the heart to Dharma.
Location: Big Bear Retreat Center
Chapters: 00:00 Settling on sound 01:16 Soft gaze inward 03:09 Weight and ease 07:48 The guarded territory 12:39 Lay down your arms 15:39 Contracting and opening 20:21 Life happening 27:13 No preference 36:49 Closing reflection
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/91354/

item.title: Guided Meditation: The Pull of Time
item.description:
The practice begins by softening what can be softened, and then turns toward whatever tension, pain, or grasping remains without trying to force it away. From there, the talk moves through care for the body, the felt transmission of kindness, and a steady reminder that attention is often entangled with wanting and fear. A central turn comes with the recognition that we cannot control experience, so the practice is to let go and receive this moment; later, the talk widens that into a view that insight into one of the Four Noble Truths can be on offer in any moment.
Chapters: 00:12 Settling and Relaxing 01:37 Tending the Body 03:06 Care and Lineage 05:20 Practice and Human Longing 09:48 Wanting and Fear 15:16 Letting Go to Receive 21:02 Insight on Offer 26:36 Not Missing It
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/22694

item.title: Guided Meditation: Relax and Let go
item.description:
Begin by settling the body and taking a few deeper breaths, then let the breath become less of a project and more of a place to rest. The talk moves from gentle posture instructions into a quiet argument for renouncing the impulse to solve life, with the poignancy of mortality helping that letting go feel possible. From there, the practice keeps returning to what is restful—the settled body, silence, the space behind closed eyes, and the soothing of Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha—before opening into long stretches of simple counting and breath awareness.
Chapters: 00:00 Arriving to meditate 00:52 Posture and breath 02:48 Letting settle 05:14 Releasing the urge 07:31 What feels restful 09:08 Resting with practice 13:28 Receiving the breath 15:12 Counting practice
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/22671

item.title: Guided Meditation: Relaxing the tension called 'me'
item.description:
What happens when the tension called “me” is not argued with, but gently set down? This guided meditation moves from settling into posture and sensing the body to noticing how control is built through feelings, images, words, and subtle plans. The practice keeps returning to relaxation, surrender, and a simple wakefulness that can hold uncertainty without reaching for grip.
Chapters: 00:13 Settling In 01:28 Touching Tranquility 04:01 Relax What Can 05:24 Self as Headquarters 09:38 Seeing the Mechanics 15:16 From Control to Wakefulness 23:44 Contraction and Uncertainty 25:00 Habitualizing Impermanence
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/22669

item.title: Guided Meditation: Stillness and the Dissolution of the Predictive Mind
item.description:
How do you meet a mind that keeps leaning ahead into prediction? The talk begins as a guided settling: relaxing the face, jaw, shoulders, belly, and breath, then resting in the simple pleasure of breathing and aliveness. From there it turns toward the subtle habit of expecting the next moment to be “me” again, and gently explores how meditation can loosen that predictive architecture and let attention fall into the present.
Chapters: 00:01 Opening and settling in 01:31 Doing much less 02:48 Softening the body 07:06 Grounding and openness 10:38 Breath and aliveness 16:48 Predicting the future 19:46 Samadhi and craving 23:38 Falling into presence
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/22663

item.title: Guided Meditation: Much Becomes Incidental
item.description:
What changes when you stop trying to build, manage, or improve experience, and simply listen? This guided meditation keeps returning to silence as something to attune to directly, even when noise and thought are still present. From that ground, pleasant and unpleasant, personal story, and even control itself are invited to become incidental for a little while, so the practice can rest in receiving the moment. The movement is toward surrender: no projects, no self-making, just space receiving space, with the suggestion that if anything is built, let it be love.
Chapters: 00:13 Listening to silence 01:58 What becomes incidental 03:43 Receiving the moment 06:41 No projects, no building 08:29 Surrender and love 09:27 Receiving space 16:54 A new chance to let go 20:18 Emerging unchanged
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/22668

From that ground, pleasant and unpleasant, personal story, and even control itself are invited to become incidental for a little while, so the practice can rest in receiving the moment. The movement is toward surrender: no projects, no self-making, just space receiving space, with the suggestion that if anything is built, let it be love.
Chapters: 00:13 Listening to silence 01:58 What becomes incidental 03:43 Receiving the moment 06:41 No projects, no building 08:29 Surrender and love 09:27 Receiving space 16:54 A new chance to let go 20:18 Emerging unchanged
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/22668

item.title: Guided Meditation: Reverence & Renunciation
item.description:
How do reverence and renunciation meet in the actual moment of practice? The talk begins with posture, breath, and a sense of the ordinary becoming sacred, then moves into the idea that meditation is not just personal self-improvement but participation in a lineage of sincerity, wisdom, and compassion. From there, it turns to renunciation as a simple putting-down: of multitasking, worry, grasping, and the urge to make a story out of every sense impression, so breathing can become more intimate and life less complicated.
Chapters: 00:00 Settling In 00:55 Posture as Reverence 03:24 Breath and Softening 05:00 Sacred Lineage 09:55 What to Put Down 13:54 Story and Hope 18:20 Clinging Simplifies 24:16 Resolution and Bells
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/22659

item.title: Guided Meditation: All Phenomena are Friendly
item.description:
The talk begins by asking what happens if we stop turning experience into a problem to be assessed, and instead let the body sit, breathe, and soften. It moves through posture, the pressure of self-making, and the way uncertainty can feel like too much responsibility, then uses the breath as a place to begin again without tension. Near the end, the phrase “all phenomena are friendly” becomes the main lens: not that everything is pleasant, but that experience can be met with less fear and less resistance, even when something hurts.
Chapters: 00:12 Arrival and settling 00:53 Letting go of selfing 02:49 Breath and fresh space 07:26 Pressure to perform 11:55 Uncertainty and release 17:42 Precision without tension 23:19 All phenomena friendly
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/22655

item.title: Guided Meditation: Arriving and Opening
item.description:
The talk begins by asking for a kind of arriving that does not rush to force the heart or attention into a fixed shape. From there it gently scans posture, body image, self-consciousness, and places that hurt, inviting relaxation, softness, and a little patience with whatever is showing up. The middle settles into repeated breath awareness, then opens into a simple tension: the mind keeps foraging for scraps of peace, while the teaching points toward relinquishment and a satisfaction that actually nourishes.
Chapters: 00:12 Arriving Gently 01:52 Sensing What Needs Care 05:02 Relaxing Self-Image 07:55 Breathing With Safety 12:45 Relinquishment and Nourishment 13:53 Extended Silent Practice 20:17 Meeting Agitation
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/22607

item.title: Guided Meditation: Mostly Quiet Sit
item.description:
If you cannot stop awareness, the talk begins, rest there: it turns the meditation away from force and toward what is already present. From there it moves through possible anchors in breath, body, soundscape, and feeling tone, then keeps returning to a simple refrain: soften, widen, relax. Later, the speaker invites sound itself to pass through the head without making inner speech special, and closes by linking careful attention with love, so that what is heavy can become lighter and more spacious.
Chapters: 00:00 Rest with awareness 04:48 Choose an anchor 06:54 Ease the selfing 09:56 Soften, widen, relax 24:42 Include inner sound 30:09 Heavy becomes light
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/22625

item.title: Guided Meditation: Life without Words
item.description:
What would your life feel like if you had no words? The meditation keeps returning to that question as the talk loosens the habit of naming, narrating, and categorizing each moment. From there it moves through craving, urgency, and the pull to control, then opens into silence, stillness, and the sense that nothing needs to be acquired or proved. A brief Buddhist reference to “give up all that is not yours” sharpens the inquiry into ownership and self-making, before the practice settles into simply remaining present.
Chapters: 00:01 Opening question 01:46 Meaning and narration 04:31 Sense experience 06:18 Fewer words 11:29 Working with urgency 15:42 Silence pervades 24:24 Give up ownership 36:44 Stillness as object 40:20 Closing and transition
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/22620

item.title: Guided Meditation: Being Held, Settling In
item.description:
What if the first task is simply to let yourself be held—by rain, by the earth, by the fact of being here? The talk begins with that sense of support and then moves into a guided settling with the breath, noticing the cycle of in-breath and out-breath, false alarms of urgency, and the ways suffering can be met without obeying it. Later it turns toward walking practice, boredom, and the quiet possibility that goodness is building even when little seems to be happening.
Chapters: 00:00 Feeling Held 02:01 Settling In 08:17 Letting Go Of Time 10:41 Breath Cycle 15:08 False Alarms 22:16 Working With Urgency 31:03 Suffering As Dukkha 39:51 Walking Meditation 47:41 Fresh Anchors
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/22614

item.title: Guided Meditation: Peace is Close
item.description:
A quiet guided practice begins with a few deeper breaths, then asks us to make room for worries without getting pulled into them. The talk repeatedly returns to the gesture of offering the heart to this moment, laying down the project of life, and breathing without elaboration. As the practice settles, it turns toward subtle shifts in attention, the softening of self-construction, and a question of what remains when we stop taking ourselves as an object.
Chapters: 00:14 Arriving Together 00:51 Landing In Breath 02:45 Putting Worries Down 05:06 Offering The Heart 07:04 Breathing Without Elaboration 12:07 Small Shifts Matter 17:59 Constructing Self 22:07 Unseen And Empty
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/22488

item.title: Guided Meditation: Sangha & Self
item.description:
What if the thing that blocks waking up is the feeling that we should be able to do it alone? This guided meditation begins with a quiet reflection on Sangha as something essential rather than secondary, using Socratic dialogue and the “jewel of Sangha” to point toward mutual dependence, shared inquiry, and the relief of not having to hold everything by oneself. The talk then settles into practice, softening into breath and collective goodwill while noticing how self-view shapes what seems solid, until even the sense of “I am” is treated as something that can be seen, loosened, and made empty.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening and context 03:19 Open Socrates 05:53 The jewel of Sangha 06:58 Mutual dependence 10:44 Seeing self-view 19:46 Noticing the vantage point 24:25 Subtle images and words
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/22405

item.title: Guided Meditation: Abiding in Goodness
item.description:
What happens when kindness and compassion are treated not as extras, but as necessities? This guided meditation keeps returning to that question by inviting a direct reconnection with love, especially when the heart feels dry, brittle, or tired by hatred and misattunement. The practice moves from resting in an image, word, or felt sense of love, to recognizing the shared longing of all beings to be well, and finally to a body-based balance of tenderness and courage. Along the way, the talk leans on the phrases "strong back, soft front" and the idea that love sometimes feels warm and bright, and sometimes simply makes space for whatever needs to be felt.
Chapters: 00:12 Love as Necessity 06:17 Abiding in Love 12:22 Self and Others 18:29 Grief and Courage 23:03 Deep Faith in Love 26:15 Closing Refrain
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/22332

item.title: Guided Meditation: Freeing Experience
item.description:
What if freedom is not something the self gets, but something experience is freed from? The talk begins with that idea and then turns into a guided meditation on widening awareness, settling the body, and letting images, sounds, feelings, pain, and thoughts take their place without clinging. Again and again, it returns to the question of how much can be released in the breath, so that experience becomes less owned, less tense, and more impersonal.
Chapters: 00:25 Freedom Reframed 01:20 Settling the Body 03:23 Including What Appears 08:37 Freeing Experience 13:45 Less Possession 15:39 Impersonal Arising 24:28 Equanimity and Control 25:57 Resting in Awareness
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/22215

item.title: Guided Meditation: Resting in Goodness
item.description:
The talk starts from a simple instruction: let goodness itself soothe the heart. From there, it moves through posture, attention, and the habit of making meditation into a performance, then loosens that pressure by inviting forgiveness toward the mind and trust in awareness and love. The emphasis keeps returning to resting rather than striving, and to noticing that what is already good and enjoyable in experience can be felt more directly.
Chapters: 00:12 Settling In 01:05 Goodness as Soothe 03:14 Attention and Agitation 05:50 Pressure on Self 08:19 Meditation by Itself 13:46 Clinging and Aliveness 25:05 Enjoy What Can Be
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/22137

item.title: Guided Meditation: Grounding and Opening
item.description:
The talk begins with simple instructions to settle in, then keeps returning to the body as the place where attention can land: posture, belly, bones, gravity, and the feeling of “taking your seat.” A repeated image of the Buddha touching the earth becomes the central grounding move, shifting practice away from the head and heart and back into embodied presence. From there, the guidance widens to include emotional activation, letting it be bathed in awareness and patience rather than measured against a plan for how life or meditation should unfold. Near the end, the emphasis softens into a release of performance, preference, and the need to get it right.
Chapters: 00:12 Beginning Together 00:30 Posture and Belly 02:25 Touching the Earth 04:53 Settling and Listening 08:53 Affect in the Body 11:22 Dropping Expectations 12:57 Open Practice 19:04 No Performance
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/22100

item.title: Guided Meditation: Putting Down the World
item.description:
What happens if practice asks us to stop trading this moment for a better one? The talk centers on a radical gesture of "putting down the world"—setting down greed, distress, autobiography, and the compulsion to manage time or outcome—while noticing the resistance that can feel like abandonment. From that stillness, the emphasis shifts to a fuller heart, clearer action, and a more intimate love for the world as it is.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening reflection 02:50 Settling the posture 04:03 Putting the world down 06:40 Autobiography and care 07:59 Simple immediacy 11:58 No bartering 15:08 Falling into the present 22:11 Love after letting go
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/22045

item.title: Guided Meditation: Breathing into Compassion
item.description:
Starting from James Baldwin’s idea that pain matters only insofar as it can connect us with everyone else alive, the talk turns personal suffering into the ground of compassion. From there, the practice moves into a guided settling with breath and body: sensing the body as a field, easing attention, and letting breathing unify what feels divided or excluded. The emphasis stays simple and steady—rest with sensation, let pain become less private, and allow compassion to widen without forcing the details.
Chapters: 00:25 Baldwin and pain 03:38 Sense the body 06:46 Breath across the body 09:53 Simple resting 13:38 Pain as connection 16:12 Compassion opens wider 22:50 Breath as love 26:58 Shared life and closing
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/22016

item.title: Guided meditation
item.description:
The talk begins with settling the body and finding whatever in the moment feels even slightly good or soothing—especially the breath, the pause, and the relief of simply stopping. From there, it explores how equanimity lets attention rest without immediately turning toward worry, planning, or trying to rearrange the world, and how that makes room for a more open, less snagged way of being with experience. The practice keeps widening: the heart is offered to the moment, the aperture of attention opens, and what felt heavy can start to feel lighter and more spacious.
Chapters: 00:29 Settling the body 01:17 Finding what feels good 02:54 Less urgency, more equanimity 07:18 Breath as refuge 08:12 Mindfulness and letting go 11:56 Offering the heart up 17:00 Widening attention 24:03 Lightness and space 27:50 Keep opening
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/21934
