item.title: Guided Meditation: Mostly Quiet Sit
item.description:
This guided sit moves from settling the body into a quiet, spacious attention that does not force experience to become anything else. The talk keeps returning to simple instructions: attend where it feels appropriate, let go, and not be afraid of silence. Along the way, the speaker softens the practice with the image of the sangha as a field of goodwill, then closes by framing the whole sit as a kind of radical permission for conditions to be as they are, without turning that into passivity in every moment.
Chapters: 01:24 Settling In 03:34 Personalizing Practice 09:39 Letting Go 16:08 Sangha Support 21:44 Radical Permission 27:17 Closing Bells
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/21888

item.title: Guided Meditation: Self-acceptance
item.description:
How do you stop sorting yourself into acceptable and unacceptable parts long enough to meet your life with kindness? The talk moves from a short settling into a clear invitation to rest in the body, then widen into the sense that a person is shaped by countless forces—history, conditioning, joy, and sorrow—that cannot be fully controlled. From there it turns toward a radical yes to experience: not to excuse harm or avoid change, but to let acceptance end shame and make room for compassion, equanimity, and a simple blessing practice of “may I be happy, may I be safe, may I be at peace.”
Chapters: 01:52 Settling In 04:04 Body and Awareness 06:23 Kindness for Conditioning 09:12 Accepting My Life 15:22 A Radical Yes 17:59 End the Inner Game 26:23 Loving-Kindness Closing
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/21873

item.title: Guided meditation: Affect and the Lure of Thought
item.description:
How do you stop giving thought the last word when it feels like it is holding loose ends, emotions, and unfinished business together? This guided meditation moves between a gentle settling and a sharper "start on a dime" stance, inviting a quick renunciation of distraction and a willingness to feel the vulnerability that can show up when thinking is no longer used as a pacifier. The practice keeps returning to the body, emotional activation, and equanimity, showing how urgency softens when attention stays close to direct experience.
Chapters: 00:12 Settling into practice 00:40 Start on a dime 01:43 Putting thoughts down 04:30 Renunciation as feeling 06:36 Body and emotion 15:51 Equanimity and urgency 17:54 This is your life 23:36 Release of self
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/21869

item.title: Guided Meditation: Attention and Love
item.description:
This guided meditation begins by settling again and again, then drops attention into the chest and belly to feel the body as grounded, rooted, and in contact with the earth. From that steadiness, the talk turns toward a quieter question: how attention can reveal what we love, what matters, and how to meet life with care rather than aversion. The practice ends by filling out the body with heart energy, softening into well-wishing, and a direct reminder to breathe and feel love instead of feeding hatred.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening and Settling 02:18 Arriving in the Body 04:01 Chest, Breath, Belly 08:24 Earth and Rootedness 11:44 Attention Reveals Love 15:09 Patience with Longing 17:23 Heart Energy in the Body 21:43 Care and the Wider Field 26:59 Love over Hatred
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/21866

item.title: Guided Meditation: What can you stop doing?
item.description:
What if love is not another kind of wanting, but a way of stepping outside wanting and fear? The talk moves from that question into a clear distinction between clinging and metta, describing loving-kindness as a practice that makes the mind vast, approaches what is usually avoided, and lets some pain unwind. It also keeps the realism of ordinary attachment in view, especially in family and romantic life, asking whether we can consent to the dukkha built into love without reaching for a fantasy of perfect non-attachment. By the end, the practice widens into a quiet kind of moral elevation: becoming moved by goodness, abiding in warmth, and making hatred less tenable.
Chapters: 00:16 Opening into metta 00:35 Love beyond wanting 01:49 Wanting and fear 03:29 Metta as release 04:26 Approaching what hurts 06:08 Kindness, not niceness 08:32 Attachment with grace 10:20 Love without control 12:22 Abiding in goodness
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/21856

item.title: Guided Meditation: Putting Down the Project of Your Life
item.description:
How do you stop carrying the project of your life in every moment? This guided meditation begins with settling the body, encouraging a posture that is relaxed and alert, and then keeps returning to the breath as a way to signal safety and soften defensiveness. From there, the talk repeatedly invites surrender into silence and not-knowing, suggesting that worry and self-management can be set down, at least for a while, so the present moment can be felt more simply. It ends by linking that settling to the quieter qualities the nervous system longs for: love, kindness, compassion, forgiveness, care, and peace.
Chapters: 00:12 Arriving and settling 02:45 Relaxed, alert posture 05:13 Patience with worry 08:05 Breath over vigilance 11:34 Tranquility and not knowing 15:26 Putting down the project 17:18 Returning without carrying 24:15 Wholesome qualities emerge
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/21862

item.title: Guided Meditation: It's all come to this
item.description:
This guided meditation begins by gathering up the goodness of practice and letting it seed the mind, then widening to include the whole arc of a life of meditation, pain, love, and doubt. The talk keeps returning to a quiet refrain of “gently tending to your life,” asking you to meet this moment without violence or self-protection. From there, it points toward a deeper refuge in simple contact with breath, body, and the changing winds of experience.
Chapters: 00:12 Settling In 01:07 Recollecting Practice 04:08 All Come To This 05:38 Gently Tending Life 13:58 Ordinary Strategies 16:46 Deeper Refuge 24:06 Winds Of Change
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/21824

item.title: Guided Meditation: Letting the Dharma Soak into You
item.description:
This guided meditation begins by settling into the body and the earth, then turns toward whatever tension, numbness, or distance is present with patience and quiet love. Rather than trying to force a state of calm, the talk invites a gentle tending to the sense of separation, letting awareness soften what feels remote. It gradually widens into recollection and care, using Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha to re-enter sensitivity, urgency, and a more supple heart.
Chapters: 02:16 Settling In 05:18 Body and Earth 06:50 Meeting Unsettledness 08:56 When Dharma Feels Distant 14:03 Recalling the Triple Gem 16:23 Courage To Keep Looking 23:57 Offering Without Strings
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/21803

item.title: Guided Meditation: Vitality and Release, In Breath, Out Breath
item.description:
What if the breath itself is teaching both vitality and release? The talk begins with a simple contrast between in-breath and out-breath, then turns that into a practice of learning to love both alertness and surrender: the rush of energy, the brightness of presence, and the softening that lets go of vigilance, fear, and the impulse to grasp. It keeps returning to the sense that meditation is not a rehearsal for some other life, but the direct texture of this one—breath, thought, feeling, seeing, hearing—met moment by moment with clarity and release.
Chapters: 00:14 Arriving and orienting 01:49 Vitality and surrender 03:06 Settling the body 04:50 The in-breath's vividness 07:10 Releasing the out-breath 12:21 Breath and clarity 17:31 Letting go of becoming 24:10 This is our life
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/21763

item.title: Guided Meditation: Vulnerability & Courage
item.description:
How can vulnerability be the source of real strength? The talk begins with settling the body into a relaxed but alert openness, then keeps returning to the sense of being undefended in the face of experience. From there, it draws a quiet line from tenderness and porousness to courage, even using Aristotle’s idea of courage as the middle between recklessness and cowardice to sharpen the point. The practice movement is simple and steady: breathe, relax, make the heart available, and see what changes when there is nothing left to defend.
Chapters: 00:12 Settling In 01:46 Fresh Curiosity 02:52 Breathing and Waking 06:02 Vulnerability as Strength 08:23 Undefended Heart 12:31 Attuning to Courage 16:45 Middle Way of Courage 23:15 Nothing to Defend
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/21718

item.title: Guided Meditation: Soothing, Space, Love
item.description:
The practice begins by gently noticing what is already here in the heart and body, then shifting into simple contact: a hand on the belly, a thumb and finger rubbing, attention to subtle pleasantness and warmth. From there, the talk lets the body soften and expand through the breath, making room for whatever feels braced or frozen, before opening into vastness without trying to manufacture it. The movement culminates in a spacious, almost elemental image of life as a gust of wind meeting another gust—what the talk calls love—and ends by returning fear and other arisings back to that larger field.
Chapters: 00:12 Arrival and Intention 01:09 Soothing the Body 04:24 Breath and Unfreezing 08:47 Recognizing Vastness 11:52 Space Without Struggle 15:37 Love and Poignancy 17:28 Returning to Love
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/21670

item.title: Guided Meditation: Thinking as coping
item.description:
How does thinking try to protect us when we feel unsafe? This guided meditation treats thought as a form of coping, then gently invites the body to settle, the mind to soften, and experience to be met without over-interpreting it. The talk moves from lineage and trust into simple noting, blessing imperfection, and easing the urge to elaborate or check one more time. By the end, silence is presented not as a demand, but as something that can welcome us just as we are.
Chapters: 00:12 Lineage and trust 00:52 Settling the body 03:20 Blessing imperfection 05:42 Safety and urgency 10:30 Thinking as coping 13:06 Peace with imperfection 18:59 Innocent thinking 19:59 Silence welcomes you
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/21623

item.title: Love & Rest Daylong - Tranquility, Shinzen Young's Do-Nothing Practice, Intro and Guided
item.description:
The talk begins with a question about how tranquility and insight arise together, and keeps returning to the tension between opening to life and taking enough protection to not get overwhelmed. From there it moves through concrete ways of settling attention: breath, gravity, the belly, silence, blankness behind closed eyes, and the visual field as something that arises and passes. In the second half, the teaching shifts into a do-nothing practice attributed to Shinzen Young: dropping the intention to control attention, not sorting experience into good or bad, and resting in the “effort of non-effort.”
Chapters: 00:00 Tranquility and insight 02:25 Protection and sensitivity 05:24 Why settling matters 09:52 Breath and body 12:31 Listening to silence 15:28 Do-nothing practice 22:58 Guided release 40:25 No good or bad meditation
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/21699

item.title: Guided Meditation: Stabilizing; Compassion
item.description:
Beginning with the uneasy feeling that relaxing might be neglectful, the talk gently asks what happens when vigilance and clinging are allowed to soften. It moves from settling the body, to sensing the breath in the back, numb, cold, or overactive places, until the body feels unified and suspended in awareness. From there, it turns to compassion: meeting sorrow, pain, grief, rage, and fear with a patient love that does not need to make pain disappear.
Chapters: 00:09 Settling In 01:01 Letting Go of Vigilance 02:53 Kind Intention 04:22 Breath in the Body 06:00 Unifying Numb Areas 07:53 Soothing Intensity 12:19 Turning Toward Compassion 18:42 Words of Care 24:23 Patient Love
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/21564

item.title: Guided Meditation: In the Presence of Love (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
What happens when meditation stops being a project and becomes a willingness to be held? This guided practice begins with fuller breaths, sensing a wave of awareness and love through the body, then gently notices the secret agendas that can make practice feel static: trying not to feel pain, trying to fix oneself, trying to become someone better. Again and again, the talk returns to the simple movements of surrendering to gravity, relaxing reference points, and trusting that love will catch you in the present moment.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Settling into breath 01:18 Giving over to gravity 05:14 Hidden agendas 11:17 Nothing to fix 16:09 Nontransactional awareness 21:01 Letting reference points go 30:12 Falling into love 38:27 Love in daily life
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/86617/

item.title: Guided Meditation: Relax into Alertness
item.description:
How do you stay relaxed without drifting into dullness, and alert without slipping into strain? This guided meditation keeps returning to that balance: sensing the body-mind, softening unnecessary effort, and letting attention settle so it can actually know more, feel more, and brighten. The practice moves from resting with the breath and bodily experience to noticing the mind itself—its brightness, dullness, agitation, tranquility, grasping, aversion, and peace—while trusting that simple awareness can do its own work.
Chapters: 00:09 Entering Sangha 01:02 Balancing Energy 02:55 Relaxing for Clarity 04:58 Gentle Single-Pointing 08:33 Letting Go of Worry 12:25 Putting Down Busyness 17:00 Not Missing Goodness 20:22 Watching the Mind 22:37 Stability as Object
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/21464

item.title: Guided Meditation: Gentleness & Equanimity
item.description:
What does it mean to stay with experience without pushing, pulling, or fighting? This guided meditation moves from resting in silence and breathing, into radical honesty paired with non-violence toward whatever arises, including ambivalence, pleasure, and pain. The practice keeps returning to gentleness as a kind of strength: connecting rather than controlling, and allowing awareness to simply bless its objects. By the end, the talk points to a quiet trust that we can know the mind only when we stop trying to do something with it.
Chapters: 00:12 Held by Silence 01:14 Radical Honesty 03:22 Not Fighting to Heal 07:03 Gentle Strength 10:03 Connecting, Not Controlling 14:41 Hands Soft, Clear 18:51 Knowing the Mind 21:40 Clinging and Love
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/21412

item.title: Guided Meditation: Tranquility
item.description:
How does the body settle when it has been trained to treat everything as urgent? This guided meditation keeps returning to the breath, the spine, the belly, and the felt sense of gravity, asking you to soften out of the head and into a steadier ground. From there, the practice gently works with sleepy or restless energy, moments of hyper-arousal, and the loose ends of the day, until tranquility is less an idea than a shared field the body can inhabit. When there is even a thread of pleasantness, the talk suggests following it and letting ease and tranquility feed each other.
Chapters: 00:12 Settling in 01:20 Posture and gravity 02:58 Dropping to the belly 04:47 Softening the headspace 07:31 Working with arousal 10:38 Urgency and false alarms 15:58 Accountability without blame 21:46 Following pleasantness
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/21295

item.title: Guided Meditation: Concentration, Clarity, Equanimity
item.description:
What changes when the point of meditation is not to chase the right object, but to refine the quality of attention itself? This guided practice begins with gratitude and then opens into three linked capacities: concentration as stability, clarity as higher-resolution perception, and equanimity as a less reactive, more frictionless relationship to what arises. The talk keeps returning to the breath, the body, and sound as possible anchors, while encouraging a steady, undaunted rehearsal of freedom moment by moment.
Chapters: 00:12 Opening Gratitude 03:37 What Attention Needs 05:25 Higher-Resolution Clarity 06:54 Equanimity and Frictionlessness 13:17 Practice Moment by Moment 17:11 Rehearsing Freedom 21:36 Open-Ended Release
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/21258

item.title: Guided Meditation: Effort as Not-Self
item.description:
What happens if effort itself is part of the problem? This guided meditation begins by asking for a minute of not doing, then invites a simple experiment: stop whatever can be stopped, and notice how often meditation turns into self-efforting. From there, the practice shifts toward relaxing the urge to control, receiving the breath openly, and sourcing attention from something deeper, less harsh, and less compulsive.
Chapters: 00:00 Settling and Stopping 00:43 Freedom Already Here 02:36 How Effort Becomes Self 05:06 Relaxing the Impulse 09:36 Receiving the Breath 14:15 Choosing the Right Counsel
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/21242

item.title: Guided Meditation: Intimacy with Breathing
item.description:
Beginning with the feeling of a familiar breath, this guided meditation turns attention toward how breathing can be surprisingly fresh, immediate, and intimate. The talk repeatedly invites a softer, closer contact with the sensations of breathing, noticing the tiny gaps, the changing rhythms, and the way attention can settle when it comes home to the breath. When the mind gets sticky or pulled into thought, the practice returns to the body and the simple steadiness of breathing together.
Chapters: 00:00 Arriving With Breath 00:56 Images and Sensation 03:25 Thin Slices of Time 06:10 Breath Awakens the Senses 07:45 Peace and Refuge 13:29 Forgiving the Mind 15:28 Home in the Breath 19:14 Sticky Thoughts to Breath
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/21240

The talk repeatedly invites a softer, closer contact with the sensations of breathing, noticing the tiny gaps, the changing rhythms, and the way attention can settle when it comes home to the breath. When the mind gets sticky or pulled into thought, the practice returns to the body and the simple steadiness of breathing together.
Chapters: 00:00 Arriving With Breath 00:56 Images and Sensation 03:25 Thin Slices of Time 06:10 Breath Awakens the Senses 07:45 Peace and Refuge 13:29 Forgiving the Mind 15:28 Home in the Breath 19:14 Sticky Thoughts to Breath
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/21240

item.title: Guided Meditation: Breath, Pleasure, Opening
item.description:
The talk begins with a small shift of the face and breath: a slight smile, a felt ripple of ease, and the invitation to notice how joy can coexist with difficulty. From there it stays close to the body, asking the listener to relax what can be relaxed, let tension remain if it must, and find something subtly pleasant to rest attention on. The practice gradually widens from the breath to the whole field of awareness, with an emphasis on staying open, trusting awareness, and not making suffering into the whole story.
Chapters: 00:10 Settling with a Smile 01:40 Joy with Difficulty 03:56 Relaxing and Receiving 08:13 Breath and Pleasure 12:57 An Anchor Everywhere 14:45 Opening to Awareness 20:41 Letting Instructions Go
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/21233

item.title: Guided Meditation: Not Predicting Me
item.description:
What happens when the mind keeps predicting the next moment, and the self feels so familiar it turns invisible? This guided meditation turns toward that habit of self-story and asks what it would be like to meet experience without expecting to find exactly the same person again. The practice moves from noticing the song of “me” to letting it become an object of awareness, then relaxing into breathing and a wider, quieter wakefulness.
Chapters: 00:00 Arrival 01:00 Predicting Ourselves 02:57 Opening to Not-Knowing 06:05 The Song of Self 10:46 Love It to Death 12:59 Settling Into Breath 25:21 Global Wakefulness
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/21224

Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/21224

item.title: Guided Meditation: Relaxing into the Moment
item.description:
What happens if the mind stops treating the present moment as a problem to solve and instead meets it with breath and trust? This guided meditation begins with settling into fuller breathing, then repeatedly returns to the simple reassurance that it is okay to be here, like this, now. From there, the practice widens into awareness of the front and back of the body, the grounding weight of gravity, and a steadier way of resting with whatever is pleasant or unpleasant. The talk closes by pointing toward a refuge in sensory clarity and a heart-mind less caught in the struggle with experience.
Chapters: 00:12 Honesty With the Moment 01:04 Settling With Breath 03:31 Relaxing the Problem Habit 06:24 Body as Refuge 08:56 Soothing and Energizing 12:00 Dissolving Worry 17:07 Finding Refuge 25:35 What Truly Matters
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/21219

item.title: Guided Meditation: Relaxing into Love
item.description:
The talk begins with a simple turn: to contact the heart, first relax. From there it guides a balance of easing and uprightness, fuller breathing, and a gradual softening until awareness can take on the hue of love and kindness. The practice moves through body, heart center, and repeated phrases such as “May I be well” and “May all beings be well,” including room for pain, self-forgiveness, and a willingness to feel joy and sorrow without contracts with the moment.
Chapters: 00:00 Settling and posture 01:25 Breath as softening 03:41 Awareness with love 05:32 Heart posture 10:09 Bathing pain in care 14:49 Lovingkindness phrases
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/21198

item.title: Saturday Guided Sit
item.description:
A guided sit that begins by asking you to notice the heart’s inner posture and then ease off the habit of “doing” meditation. The practice moves through body awareness, the felt boundary between body and space, and the difference between arousal and valence—how feeling tones color experience without needing to become a story or action plan. From there, the talk turns toward receiving experience with clarity and equanimity, using even difficult sensations as invitations to let go and stay close to the path.
Chapters: 00:01 Settling the posture 04:04 Whole-body awareness 10:15 Arousal and valence 13:47 Clarifying feeling 18:13 Receiving without counsel 24:57 The self-story loosens 29:51 Feeling as practice 41:33 Retreat and closing notes
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/21175

item.title: Thursday Guided Meditation
item.description:
This guided meditation begins by checking for fear, bracing, and the quiet apprehension that can show up at the start of practice, then meets whatever is there with kindness rather than pressure. From there, the talk settles into breath as an anchor: noticing where breathing is clearest, feeling how it can soothe agitation and enliven numbness, and letting attention widen through the whole body. It also names the mind’s innocent habit of moving into memory and anticipation, and invites a softer relation to effort, curiosity, and the pull to control. Near the end, the emphasis turns toward surrendering into the present and letting scattered intentions line up through release.
Chapters: 00:01 Arriving with fear 02:56 Common practice difficulties 04:37 Finding the breath 09:13 Breath through the body 14:06 Willingness and curiosity 21:51 Grounded openness 28:02 Memory and anticipation 37:36 Letting intentions align
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/21146

item.title: Formal Opening Part 1 (sit)
item.description:
A short opening to the retreat, beginning not with teaching but with a 15-minute sit and the simple intention to bless the retreat by sitting together. From there the talk moves into practical setup: cushions, chairs, hearing devices, the daily rhythm of silent sitting and walking, and how instruction, talks, reflection, and practice discussions will be arranged. It ends with a gentle reminder that the week itself has been set aside for retreat, and that this shared time is already part of the practice.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the retreat 15:10 Comfort and seating 16:32 Daily meditation rhythm 16:58 Instruction and talks 17:08 Practice groups 17:35 Bell practice 18:05 Closing encouragement
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/21138

item.title: Guided Meditation: Feeling and Verbal Thinking
item.description:
How can a meditation practice stay open when it keeps being pulled toward fixing, explaining, and controlling? The talk begins by questioning the impulse to reach for technique, then settles into feeling the body directly: its pressure, arousal, and shifting emotional textures, with attention and patience instead of defense. From there it turns to inner speech, treating thought as something that can be heard rather than obeyed, and finally to the way feeling and story shape each other. The practice movement is simple and sustained: notice, allow, and let both feeling and words be held in awareness with less compulsion.
Chapters: 00:00 Arriving and Settling 02:40 Letting Go of Technique 05:44 Breath and Release 07:45 Feeling in the Body 11:02 Patience and Permission 14:19 Hearing Thoughts 19:18 Feelings and Stories 23:56 Equanimity and Blessing
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/21104

item.title: Guided Meditation: You cannot know oneself while fixing oneself
item.description:
The meditation keeps returning to a simple refusal: not to turn practice into another project of fixing the self. It begins with posture, grounding, and the breath, then repeatedly lets go of willfulness in favor of surrender, silence, and the body breathing on its own. From there the talk widens into noticing fears, thoughts, and phenomena as empty and wisp-like, while meeting whatever arises with patience rather than control.
Chapters: 00:36 Settling the body 03:59 Breath and ease 05:58 Putting down the project 09:14 Simple, beckoning practice 15:41 Phenomena and fear 17:23 Wisp-like experience 23:28 Silence and patience
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/21063

item.title: Guided Meditation: Pervasiveness of Craving
item.description:
This guided meditation starts with the felt tension between alertness and ease, using posture and breath to notice how craving and control create waves of strain. The practice then turns toward surrender: not trying to make experience exact, but relaxing into the life that is actually here, with its imperfection and heartache. Again and again, the talk asks whether awareness can be trusted to receive what comes and let it become wisdom.
Chapters: 00:56 Settling Into Posture 03:21 Noticing Holding On 05:59 Surrendering to This Moment 09:03 Breath as Pleasure 13:12 Responsibility Without Power 15:30 Trusting Awareness 20:21 Receiving Wisdom
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/20987

item.title: Guided Meditation: Too Busy Knowing
item.description:
What changes when the mind stops patrolling experience and instead lets life be felt directly? This guided meditation starts with relaxing whatever can be relaxed, then asks for a deeper relinquishment of vigilance: assuming safety for a little while, surrendering to sensations, and using the breath, sound, or body as an anchor. The practice keeps returning to clarity and high-resolution attention, with impermanence as a cue to notice how experience snags and then lets go.
Chapters: 00:12 Settling and Relaxing 04:10 Safety and Relinquishment 06:56 Anchors and Clarity 09:23 Snagged by Experience 10:07 Moment by Moment Knowing 20:58 Too Busy Knowing
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/20921

item.title: Guided Meditation: Sitting in the Bones
item.description:
The practice begins by softening the breath and letting attention settle lower, as if awareness can pour down into the body rather than stay caught in the head. From there, the talk keeps returning to the felt sense of sitting in the bones: heaviness without dullness, groundedness, steadiness, and a trust that the body can simply rest in gravity. That quiet stability opens into a wider invitation to stop doing, relax the small self, and let life arise and cease with more ease. Near the end, the emphasis turns toward love and metta, suggesting that even a tranquil body can become a way of radiating loving-kindness.
Chapters: 00:12 Settling the Breath 02:30 Sitting in the Bones 04:11 Gravity and Earth 06:01 Attention Downward 09:34 Mountain Stability 11:01 Stop Doing So Much 17:28 The Self Can Relax 23:11 From Rest to Metta
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/20864

item.title: Guided Meditation: Landing in the Breath and Soundscape
item.description:
Fear is treated as the first thing to meet here: the talk begins by naming how bracing, worry, and the urge to get through practice can narrow the body and mind. From there it moves into a quiet sequence of instructions—softening with kindness, settling into the breath, opening to sound, and learning to stay awake through boredom, overwhelm, or even a “meltdown” without turning experience into a problem. Walking practice becomes a place to rotate attention across seeing, hearing, the feet, the whole body, and even love, with the repeated reminder that willingness matters more than force.
Location: Big Bear Retreat Center
Chapters: 00:01 Settling fear 04:29 Mood in awareness 10:03 Breath as refuge 17:02 Thinking without struggle 24:06 Awake and relaxed 29:59 Listening to soundscape 37:50 Retreat rhythm 45:25 Whole-day awareness 52:57 Closing encouragement
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/85473/

item.title: Guided Meditation: Non-tension
item.description:
How do you stop fighting conditions that are already here? This guided meditation works with discomfort, bracing, and the urge to wait for things to be better, inviting a simple release into whatever is present. The practice moves through sensing the body, softening tension, and letting awareness settle more fully into change and impermanence. It ends with a repeated image of falling into the present, with less baggage and fewer places for attention to snag.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening and welcome 01:45 Temperature and the body 04:13 Not waiting for perfect 12:05 Awareness in the body 14:54 Relaxing clinging 18:52 Impermanence as practice 23:28 Falling into presence
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/20758

item.title: Guided Meditation: Relaxing and Falling Awake
item.description:
This guided meditation begins with a simple instruction: find a posture that balances relaxation and alertness, then notice what is still carrying the day. The practice moves through feeling the residue of stress, offering it a blessing rather than tightening around it, and returning to the exhale, faint sounds, and the felt edge between sound and silence. Over and over, the talk points to the same turn: relaxing not by force, but by softening the willfulness that keeps holding on.
Chapters: 00:29 Settling Into Posture 01:51 Laying Down the Day 04:19 Blessing and Release 06:17 Breath and Sound 11:05 Tension and Holding 15:37 Relinquishing Control 21:07 Relaxing as Exhale 24:30 Falling Awake
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/20693

item.title: Guided Meditation: Relaxation and the Tension of Dukkha, Self, Clinging
item.description:
What if the way into practice is not more effort, but a deeper and more honest relaxing? The talk moves from a small image of a tired child asking for the bed that is already there, into a sustained inquiry into how clinging, selfing, greed, hatred, and delusion show up as tension in the body-mind. Through breath, gravity, and repeated softening, the speaker suggests that when attention keeps releasing its grip, what has been held by suffering can finally be felt and let go.
Chapters: 00:14 Opening story 01:43 Relaxation and suffering 05:36 Right here to sit 07:23 Let tension speak 10:21 Gravity and ease 13:35 Self as contraction 18:28 Clinging and hate 21:42 Delusion and mortality 24:15 Putting things down
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/20627

item.title: Guided Meditation: Self and Willfulness
item.description:
The talk begins by asking what it means to start meditating at all, then quickly turns toward a harder question: can you stop doing the many things you think you need to do? From there, the practice keeps loosening the grip of willfulness, inviting trust in awareness, in the body, and in what is already present without being managed. Rather than building a technique, the guidance repeatedly asks the listener to put efforts down, notice suspicion of self, and let doing dissolve into openness.
Chapters: 00:00 Settling In 01:00 What Began Meditation 04:07 Trusting the Mind 06:31 The Self as Organizer 08:09 Anchor Everywhere 12:17 Willfulness and Job 15:25 Awareness Finds Us 21:44 Surrender and Ease
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/20585

item.title: Guided Meditation: Tranquility
item.description:
How do you let the mind settle without forcing it, and then use that settling to see more clearly? This guided meditation moves gently through the body and breath, asking you to elongate the breathing, notice what is already resting, and let tension drain rather than be managed by effort. From there it opens into listening for silence, resting with the visual field behind closed eyes, and softening the habit of clinging long enough to return to stillness.
Chapters: 00:09 Tranquility and clarity 01:56 Resting the body 05:18 Softening clinging 07:28 Body as a resource 10:54 Listening for silence 14:59 Silent listening 17:37 Visual field and darkness 23:27 Letting clinging wait 25:50 Closing rest
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/20530

item.title: Guided Meditation: Goodnight Moon
item.description:
A children’s bedtime book becomes the frame for this meditation: the repeated act of saying goodnight to memories, pain, thoughts, feelings, and plans. The talk moves gently from that image into a guided sitting, with deep breaths, a soothing out-breath, and a practice of putting down whatever has been lingering from the day or week. It keeps returning to the question of how to release experience without bracing against it, so what remains can rest in a wider, quieter field.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the Meditation 02:36 Saying Goodnight 09:15 Settling With Breath 11:49 Open Awareness 15:04 Not Rejection 16:08 Resting in Presence 26:02 Fatigue and Brightness
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/20468

item.title: Guided Meditation: Feeling
item.description:
What happens if feeling is not treated as a problem to fix, but as the field where reactivity reveals itself and can unwind? This guided meditation begins with breath and bodily sensation, then moves through the way feeling shapes sleepiness, restlessness, craving, and doubt, inviting a simpler seeing that does not keep narrating everything into a story. From there, the practice turns toward relinquishment, non-thinking, and a quieter kind of effort—one that can rest in the present moment without forcing it.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Settling With Breath 02:14 Problems and Dissolving 03:10 Commitment To This Moment 06:25 Wanting And Knowing 09:46 Feeling And Reactivity 13:46 Stories From Feeling 20:12 Non-Thinking And Release 23:11 Clinging And Heaven 28:35 Futurelessness And Death
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/83891/

item.title: Guided Meditation: Convergence of Insight and Love (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
How does mindfulness open into love, and how does love make mindfulness bearable? This guided meditation moves steadily from the careful deconstruction of experience into the four Brahma-viharas—loving-kindness, compassion, sympathetic joy, and equanimity—showing how each one meets a different face of daily life. The practice then turns inward with simple offerings and phrases like “May I be happy” and “May my heart be softened rather than hardened by difficulty,” letting the listener rest in a wider, less self-referential heart.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Insight and love 05:13 The four abodes 08:29 Compassion and equanimity 12:40 Letting love be home 14:31 Dropping specialness 22:44 Offering goodwill 27:55 Softening into the heart
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/83872/

item.title: Guided Meditation: The Breath and Awareness don't depend on you
item.description:
The talk begins by shifting attention away from self-management: the breath is emphasized because it does not depend on what we call “me,” and awareness, too, is presented as something that cannot be started or stopped by will. From there, the practice settles into a long, quiet emphasis on emptiness, with repeated returns to the sense that experience is rising and passing without a central controller. A brief reference to stepping out of the “bureaucracy of ego” gives the teaching an image of leaving behind the micromanagement of self and resting in a more open, effortless seeing.
Chapters: 00:00 Settling In 01:15 Breath Beyond Self 03:27 Awareness Receives 05:22 Emptiness Repeated 11:07 Ego's Bureaucracy 13:08 Same Stuff, Rising 17:42 Egoic Proprioception 20:22 Less and Less Me
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/20353

item.title: Guided Meditation: Awareness and Effortlessness
item.description:
How does meditation move from effort into a silence that is already there? The talk begins with the mind’s habit of turning experience into a story about me, then uses a contemporary philosopher’s description of awareness as silent, non-reactive, and without inner agency to point toward a more immediate sense of knowing. From there, the guidance turns practical: breathe, relax what can be relaxed, soften friction with imperfection, and see how effort, choicelessness, and non-doing can gradually give way to trust and rest.
Chapters: 00:30 Narrative Self 02:23 Silent Knowing 05:07 Instructions And Fruit 07:18 Settling To Sit 09:17 Friction And Equanimity 12:32 Effort Toward Release 18:19 Trusting Silence 25:26 Resting Safely
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/20281

item.title: Guided Meditation: Language as Safety
item.description:
How much of what we call safety is really just the habit of using words to manage experience? The talk begins by questioning the common belief that thinking is the problem, and turns instead to an over-trust in language as a way of fixing unpleasantness. From there, the guided meditation asks for a sustainable posture, relaxed attention, and a willingness to rest, feel the breath, and absorb discomfort without immediately telling a story about it. By the end, the movement is toward a quieter safety: not certainty or verbal control, but wisdom, love, and the ability to stay with uncertainty.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening reflection 02:09 Settling the body 05:18 Releasing urgency 09:07 Rest without words 10:27 Breath and feeling 14:39 Uncertainty and story 19:32 Letting go into silence 25:34 Risk and deeper safety
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/20239

item.title: Guided Meditation: Attention, Effort, Choice and Selfing
item.description:
How do attention, effort, choice, and selfing fit together in meditation? The talk begins by naming the familiar work of practice—directing attention, making effort, choosing an anchor—and then turns to the way those very movements can thicken the sense of a meditator who is managing it all. From there, the instruction opens toward a quieter register: choicelessness, receptivity, and a kind of surrender that doesn’t try to defeat the self so much as see through the habit of turning practice into a project. The guided sitting follows that arc, returning to breath, gentle effort, and the possibility of letting the doer relax.
Chapters: 00:12 Opening orientation 00:42 Attention and effort 02:26 Selfing and practice 06:12 Settling with breath 09:03 Stability and reminders 14:33 Doer and deed 19:33 Surrender to grace 24:40 Beyond hierarchy
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/20193

item.title: Guided Meditation: Aperture, Vividness, Stability, Effort
item.description:
How can attention be held without turning meditation into another project of fixing, improving, or solving? The talk moves from intention—putting down greed and distress with respect to the world—into a very practical map of attention: where to place it, how wide to make the aperture, how to nurture vividness and stability, and how to adjust effort so it stays sustainable. After the instructions, the guided meditation settles into breath, body, and sound, then gradually opens into a more holistic way of noticing what is present.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening frame 01:19 Intention to release 02:28 Attention and aperture 04:34 Vividness and stability 06:01 Effort in balance 07:42 Settling into the sit 12:40 Anchor and soundscape 17:17 Returning from mind wandering 26:46 Open awareness
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/20159

item.title: Guided Meditation: What's Already Here?
item.description:
The talk opens with a simple but unsettling question: if peace and war can both come from the same human capacities, where do we actually turn to find freedom? From there, the speaker uses Ajahn Chah’s line about freedom and suffering arising in the same place to shift the whole meditation toward immediate experience. The guided practice then keeps returning to a quiet invitation: stop searching, let the breath come to you, and rest in what is already here. Over time, the sense of looking, strain, and self-doubt is meant to soften into being found by silence.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening reflection 02:25 Freedom in suffering 04:13 Settling the body 06:13 Letting breath arrive 10:39 Being found 17:10 Resting in presence 22:44 Healing the search
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/20105

item.title: Guided Meditation: Faith in Silence
item.description:
What if the mind’s constant thinking is just a form of coping, and the practice is to stop feeding it for a while? This guided meditation invites a gradual turn toward silence, with reassurance that letting go is not neglect and that the present moment does not wish to hurt you. The talk moves through settling the posture, softening the body, and resting attention in tranquility while exploring a faith that it is safe enough to release effort. Over and over, the instruction returns to listening to silence, trusting it, and letting the heart do what thought cannot.
Chapters: 00:12 Settling In 02:41 Soothing the Body 05:06 Putting Down Complications 10:18 Faith in Silence 15:52 Over-Reading Experience 18:48 Heart Training 20:05 Listening To Silence 25:20 Extended Quiet
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/20005

item.title: Guided Meditation: Lightly Guided Meditation
item.description:
The talk begins by asking for a posture of receptivity: to listen deeply enough that experience is received rather than turned into identity or resistance. From there it keeps circling the small ways meditation becomes a project of me—wanting, bargaining, control—and notices how that tightness relaxes when awareness is allowed to stay with breath, body, sound, and feeling. Near the end, a question about whether the agenda is to connect or to control opens into a quieter kind of faith: the sense that something can hold us even when our strategies fall away.
Chapters: 00:30 Settling In 01:30 Deep Listening 05:52 The Project of Me 10:01 Wanting and Agenda 14:12 Trusting Awareness 21:20 Control or Connection 25:47 Faith in Letting Go
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/19999

item.title: Guided Meditation: Attention as Prayer
item.description:
What happens when attention is taken all the way to its deepest point—past effort, past self-conscious control, and into something like prayer? The talk begins with a Simone Weil line and then unfolds into a guided meditation where body, breath, sound, and forgiveness are used to soften the sense of a meditator who is trying to manage experience. Again and again, the practice turns from clunky doing toward a quieter trust that awareness can find us, and that the usual grip of self can relax.
Chapters: 01:05 Attention as Prayer 02:53 View and Devotion 04:53 Settling the Body 07:38 Forgiveness and Recommitment 10:44 Love in Disguise 14:11 Less Control, More Grace 17:56 Letting Awareness Find Us 26:12 Groundless and Held
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/19984

item.title: Guided Meditation: Attention and Sustaining Openness
item.description:
How do you train attention without letting awareness narrow into effort or problem-solving? This guided meditation keeps returning to that tension: directing the mind toward breath, body, and sound while practicing a wider, more receptive openness when intensity, discomfort, or the urge to fix things starts to take over. The movement is gentle and practical, treating small moments of collapse as the place to notice how openness can be re-established.
Chapters: 00:00 Practice frame 02:50 Settle into posture 05:13 Intensity and collapse 09:38 Reverence for dukkha 13:43 Good enough awareness 19:47 Re-establish openness 26:31 Not solving
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/19970

item.title: Guided Meditation: Establishing Tranquility
item.description:
What changes when the mind stops bracing and narrows its field of attention? This guided meditation begins by settling into silence and posture, then asks the listener to relax what can be relaxed and allow the remaining tension to stay without struggle. From there, the practice turns toward the breath as a small, steady refuge: softening the inner gaze, feeling nourished by breathing, and noticing how even a little tranquility changes the way experience appears. Near the end, the talk points to the Buddha’s teaching that mind shapes what is experienced, using that idea to underscore the shift in view that comes with calm.
Chapters: 00:12 Settling Into Silence 02:01 Relaxing the Body 03:00 Tranquility Changes View 06:22 Narrowing Attention 11:22 Faith in Silence 20:52 Stabilizing the Inner Gaze 26:04 Tranquility as Refuge 28:22 Closing Bells
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/19960

item.title: Guided Meditation - Deep Letting Go
item.description:
If this moment had to be lived for all eternity, what posture would the heart take? The guided meditation keeps returning to that question as it invites a deep letting go of self-hatred, self-view, self-love, and self-care, then opens into a felt sense of futurelessness. From there, the practice loosens the burden of being sandwiched between memory and possibility, and explores how clinging creates the very sense of time it seems to inhabit. It ends by putting down models of the self to discover what remains when reference points and hope-fear patterns are allowed to melt.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening question 00:30 Letting go inwardly 03:56 Outside memory and future 07:04 Time and clinging 11:09 Dropping reference points 15:09 Becoming empty 21:59 Hope and fear soften 32:17 Putting down self-models
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/19756

Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/19756

item.title: Guided Meditation - Mindfulness of Feeling
item.description:
The talk begins with the rain as a way into practice: letting sound soothe the body, noticing thirst, and settling into the repeated instruction to let go. From there, it turns to feeling as the place where worry, longing, and the sense that something must happen all register first, before they harden into emotional stories. The speaker keeps returning to the felt sense in the body, showing how to stay with urgency without obeying it, and how met feeling can become a quieter relationship to experience. By the end, the emphasis has shifted from reading life as right or wrong to meeting feeling with enough wisdom that it opens into love.
Chapters: 01:00 Settling with Rain 02:47 Letting Go 06:32 Feeling as Dharma 08:20 Body and Affect 11:34 The Inner Meter 14:35 Equanimity and Soothing 20:35 Anchors and Urgency 29:51 Listening Instead of Reacting 34:30 Feeling into Love
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/19662

item.title: Guided Meditation - Landing in the Breath
item.description:
Stillness here is treated as a place where bracing, fear, and self-protection can show up, and the practice is to meet them gently rather than turn them into a story about the future. The talk moves from settling into the breath, to opening to sensations as they are, to noticing how thought tries to manage uncertainty and how easily attention can drift into narrative. It then widens into walking meditation, where the focus shifts to bodily movement, visual impressions, and the patient, almost invisible accumulation of mindfulness, with one reference to a classic teaching on the five predictable mind-body energies that can arise in retreat.
Chapters: 00:00 Settling the Heart 02:23 Thoughts as Predictions 06:21 Body Aliveness 12:38 Finding the Breath 19:38 Returning Without Judgment 26:47 Retreat Mind States 40:54 The Obstacle Is Path 47:23 Walking and Momentum 54:37 Surrender and Simplicity
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/19649

item.title: Guided Meditation: Resting In Care
item.description:
What does it mean to begin a meditation by letting the heart lead? The talk invites a simple opening: rest in some accessible form of love—gratitude, compassion, loving-kindness, joy, equanimity, even grief—and let that color the body and breath. From there, it explores how love can soften irritation, make the breath feel significant, and create a steadier container in which anger or aversion can be held without forcing them away.
Chapters: 01:06 Settling In 02:43 Begin With Love 07:25 Care Flavors Experience 09:39 Body Suffused With Care 13:45 Freedom Still Beckons 16:25 Steadier Love 22:04 Holding What Is Not Love 23:23 Closing Wish
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/19441
item.itunes:author: Matthew Brensilver
item.itunes:summary:
What does it mean to begin a meditation by letting the heart lead? The talk invites a simple opening: rest in some accessible form of love—gratitude, compassion, loving-kindness, joy, equanimity, even grief—and let that color the body and breath. From there, it explores how love can soften irritation, make the breath feel significant, and create a steadier container in which anger or aversion can be held without forcing them away.
Chapters: 01:06 Settling In 02:43 Begin With Love 07:25 Care Flavors Experience 09:39 Body Suffused With Care 13:45 Freedom Still Beckons 16:25 Steadier Love 22:04 Holding What Is Not Love 23:23 Closing Wish
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/19441

item.title: Guided Meditation: Resting In What We Cannot Stop
item.description:
This guided meditation begins with settling into a posture you can say yes to, then gently turns toward gratitude for being alive and conscious. From there, the talk explores the difference between the effort of directing attention and the quieter dimension of awareness that does not depend on will. The practice movement is to notice what is already happening, including how awareness blooms and collapses, and to rest in what cannot be stopped rather than trying to manage experience.
Chapters: 00:52 Settling In 04:00 Gratitude for Awareness 09:32 Effort and Ease 11:37 Awareness Beyond Will 20:23 Mindfulness as Grace 26:15 Resting in What Cannot Stop
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/19425

Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/19425

item.title: Guided Meditation: Letting the Moment Soften You
item.description:
What if the work of practice is less about forcing the mind into stillness and more about letting experience soften you? The talk moves through a guided meditation that alternates between easing in, landing with a breath, and gently meeting whatever is present—thought loops, anxiety, body sensations, and the places where the heart tightens. Rather than asking for special states, it keeps returning to the felt shift from clenching to release, and to the repeated practice of trusting the heart to stay available.
Chapters: 00:12 Arriving 01:13 Entering the moment 02:20 Finding the breath 03:39 Meeting the body 09:06 Softened by phenomena 11:17 When suppleness is lost 18:18 Letting go in practice 26:02 Rehearsing freedom
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/19422

item.title: Guided Meditation: Renouncing the Narrative Impulse
item.description:
What happens when feeling arises and the mind immediately turns it into a story, a diagnosis, or a command? This guided meditation keeps returning to the body and the feeling tone itself, inviting the listener to notice mood, arousal, and the urge to narrate without following that impulse. Over time, the practice softens the compulsive push to figure things out and opens a little space where nothing needs to happen right now.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening and Welcome 00:50 Inviting Goodwill 03:13 Landing in the Body 06:56 Feeling Without Story 10:23 Narrative and Vigilance 16:18 Experimenting with Defenselessness 20:50 Nothing Needs to Happen 27:46 Distilling Wisdom
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/19406

item.title: Guided Meditation: Not updating your autobiography
item.description:
What happens when meditation stops reinforcing the story you’ve been carrying about yourself? This guided practice keeps returning to the body and the breath, not to make pain pleasant, but to meet experience more precisely and let awareness spread through the whole field of sensation. From there, the talk turns toward surrender, equanimity, and not treating the present moment as the next chapter in an old autobiography.
Chapters: 00:12 Settling In 02:37 Body and Breath 06:19 Awareness Spreads 10:20 Not the Next Chapter 12:09 Letting Go of Story 19:07 Equanimity and Non-clinging 21:17 Self as Hologram 26:58 Futurelessness
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/19397

item.title: Guided Meditation - The Warmth of Love
item.description:
What if warmth is not a metaphor, but the immediate quality that lets practice soften into love? The talk moves from a few rooted images of ordinary life—walking in the rain, seeing people, noticing trees—to a sustained guided meditation on resting, allowing, and settling the mind so that kindness can touch whatever is present. It then turns toward metta, compassion, joy, and equanimity, asking what medicine fits this moment and how to remain open-hearted when suffering is not under our control.
Chapters: 00:01 Settling In 02:45 Warmth of Love 11:11 Allowing and Letting Go 24:49 What the Heart Needs 26:27 Metta and Compassion 28:24 Equanimity at Limits 30:01 Well-Wishing
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/19368

item.title: Guided Meditation - Love and Grief
item.description:
This guided meditation begins with settling the posture, then moves into a meditation on how love and grief are bound together rather than opposed. A passage from Leonard Cohen’s “Treaty” opens into a reflection on whether grief itself might be honored as a kind of practice, even a fifth Brahma-vihara, before turning toward impermanence and the possibility of grieving anicca. The talk then grounds those reflections in metta phrases and closes by linking intimate knowledge of our own suffering with a deeper compassion for others.
Chapters: 00:00 Settling the Body 07:40 Leonard Cohen's Treaty 09:09 Starting the Practice 11:42 Love and Grief 15:17 Grieving Impermanence 16:33 Opening Into Metta 21:23 Wishing Well 23:05 Compassion for Others
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/19371

item.title: Friday Morning Instructions
item.description:
What we see is not separate from what we want, so the talk keeps turning from attention to motivation: if clinging shapes knowing, then clearer seeing begins by letting go. From there, it moves through the innocence and complexity of clinging, the way feeling becomes emotion and thought, and the reminder that you are not doing your life wrong—you are just living. It ends with a quieter trust that awareness is not a story, and that when we fall, love will catch us.
Chapters: 00:01 Wanting Shapes Seeing 01:37 Letting Go 03:14 Clinging and Self-Knowledge 09:21 Affect and Innocence 17:52 Suffering and Delusion 20:42 Mind In Images 27:43 Awareness Is Not Story 29:50 Faith and Being Held
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/19240

item.title: Wednesday morning instructions
item.description:
What if experience is less a solid self than a lava lamp of blooming and collapsing thought, feeling, image, and sound? The talk moves from that opening image into a steady meditation on posture, silence, and how to meet sensation without getting locked into the story of it. From there, it traces a path from patience and receptivity to the possibility that intensity can soften into tranquility, and sometimes into love. The emphasis stays close to the body: breathing into arousal, bathing feeling in awareness, and letting the nervous system be met rather than managed.
Chapters: 00:01 Changing experience 03:19 Returning to the body 09:03 Thought as aftermath 16:14 Soothing the system 24:52 Being changed 29:12 Equanimity’s by-products 31:38 The necessity of love 44:01 Awareness and helplessness
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/19237

item.title: Morning Instructions: Landing in the body breathing
item.description:
How do you settle into practice when the mind is already tracking time, evaluating itself, and reaching for conclusions? The talk keeps returning to a very simple response: relax the face and shoulders, soften the belly, and let the breath become more prominent without force, as if attention were something that can be received rather than grabbed. From there it widens into guidance for a retreat day—moving between sitting and walking, noticing boredom, restlessness, and fatigue, and trusting continuity even when nothing seems to be happening on the surface.
Chapters: 00:01 Arriving at retreat 04:53 Soften the face 10:21 Let breath emerge 17:27 Interest over force 26:30 Restlessness and fatigue 32:59 Trust the day 43:09 Walking practice 50:31 Boredom and trust
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/19232

item.title: Reflections and Guided Meditation on Mudita
item.description:
Mudita begins where comparison tightens the heart: can joy for another’s good fortune be felt without pretending, ranking, or self-protection? The talk moves from tears and tenderness into a close look at envy, showing how measurement builds the self and how that same contraction can be used as a doorway into investigation. A guided practice follows, first with someone easy to rejoice in, then with someone more difficult, softening around the tension until rejoicing feels less like a performance and more like shared happiness.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Opening reflection 04:00 Tears and tenderness 06:19 Joy without pretending 08:54 What mudita is 12:15 Selfing through comparison 15:40 Sharing another’s good 20:31 Guided practice begins 24:53 Working with envy 29:07 Softening and closing
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/80555/

item.title: Guided meditation: all phenomena as false alarms
item.description:
What happens when every sound, feeling, and thought is treated as a false alarm rather than a message about who you are? This guided meditation begins by looking for what can simply be stopped, then turns toward careful attention to the emotional body, so urgency, fixing, and narration can soften. The talk keeps returning to the body, to letting go, and to the idea that all phenomena, held wisely, can lead to love rather than more self-story.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Settling the Doing 03:19 Care for the Heart 05:54 False Alarms 10:12 Confidence and Softening 14:54 Emotion and Urgency 21:06 No Story Needed 32:28 Love After Selfing 40:51 Questions and Closing Guidance
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/80546/

item.title: Metta Practice
item.description:
How does the heart stay open to the full intensity of experience without closing down? The talk frames metta as the support that makes insight bearable, then turns to the four brahmaviharas as distinct ways love meets goodness, suffering, joy, and the limits of what can be controlled. It moves from reflection into guided practice: settling the body, bringing to mind goodness, letting phrases steady the mind, and meeting grief or confusion with a kindness that can feel painful but releasing.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Love and Insight 02:28 Four Species of Love 06:33 Why Love Matters 10:56 Meeting What Hurts 12:45 Settling the Body 16:55 Remembering Goodness 25:32 Abiding in Kindness 31:33 Extending the Wish 37:04 A Sublime Abiding
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/80506/

item.title: Guided Meditation: Deconstructing Sensory Experience
item.description:
How do experience and selfhood loosen when seeing, hearing, and feeling are treated as separate events instead of one solid world? The talk first introduces a precise noting practice, then guides listeners through sensing visual thinking behind closed eyes, listening to the voice in the head without obeying it, and noticing emotional energy in the body as "feeling." It repeatedly returns to the idea that subtle shifts in attention can untangle the grip of identification, while also emphasizing surrender over the pressure to fix or improve practice.
Chapters: 00:12 Opening the method 01:43 Seeing, hearing, feeling 05:50 Settling and surrendering 08:28 Noticing visual thought 11:38 Listening to inner speech 17:25 Feeling in the body 19:52 Opening to all three 24:16 Cycling and untangling
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/18180

item.title: Guided Meditation: Awareness Without Willfulness
item.description:
This guided meditation begins with a simple invitation to notice what has been carried into the moment: the residues of the day, the week, the year, and the body’s own restlessness. The practice then turns toward relaxing willfulness, using the breath to smooth and unify the body-mind rather than forcing attention into place. Gradually, the talk points to a different kind of awareness—one that does not feel like “me” and does not depend on efforting to hold it together.
Chapters: 00:35 Opening candor 02:29 Settle the body 04:40 Taking stock 07:03 Allowing residue 07:59 Breath and balance 11:41 Effort and grace 17:19 Relaxing the doer 22:51 Awareness without self 30:03 Closing bells
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/18167

item.title: Guided Meditation: Listening to the Mind of Buddha
item.description:
The talk begins by inviting a shift from doing to hearing: settling into breath, body, sound, and even the darkness behind closed eyes without letting attention’s mechanics cover over the beckoning of silence. From there, a Leonard Cohen poem becomes the springboard for the instruction to listen beyond the speaker and listen to the mind of Buddha, with receptivity and trust in awareness taking the foreground. The practice then turns toward resting, forgiving the mind, and loosening the habit of sorting experience into pleasant and unpleasant, until there is nothing left to do but be awake.
Chapters: 00:11 Arriving and Settling 01:30 Silence Beckons 02:58 Simple Objects of Attention 05:04 Listening to Cohen 06:23 Trusting Awareness 15:23 Forgiveness and Rest 17:33 Patience and Urgency 23:35 Beyond Coping
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/18176

item.title: Guided Meditation: Not Locating Oneself
item.description:
How do you practice without immediately taking up the usual place of self? This guided meditation keeps returning to a simple instruction: don’t re-establish the familiar reference points of who, what, and where you are, and instead meet the moment with openness and not-knowing. Along the way, the talk briefly contrasts attention to the breath or object of practice with the way attention is held, then settles back into a sustained invitation to let self-reference dissolve into awareness.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening and welcome 00:30 Settling without knowing 01:20 Not locating a self 03:30 Opening practice 08:09 What and how 09:36 Sustained guidance 18:29 Care and openness 22:10 Closing silence
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/18177

item.title: Guided Meditation: Entering the Dharma Field
item.description:
The meditation begins by settling into posture and breath, not as a narrow technique but as a way of remembering the whole weight and beauty of practice. The speaker moves from simple attention to the breath as refuge, a place to fold in thoughts, fear, hope, anticipation, and memory until they are softened and held. In the final stretch, the practice widens into the image of entering a Dharma field: a realm where even mistakes can become reminders of Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha.
Chapters: 00:42 Arriving and settling 04:52 Breath as reminder 07:26 Opening and refuge 09:20 Breath as shelter 14:21 Folding thoughts back 18:51 Hope and fear 21:51 Invoking the Buddha 25:06 Entering Dharma field
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/18164

item.title: Guided Meditation: Trajectory of your life and trajectory of freedom
item.description:
The talk starts by setting two trajectories side by side: the path of ordinary life, with its habit of treating every present moment as evidence about the future, and the path of freedom, which asks for a different kind of response. From there it moves into a guided practice of softening around sensation, relaxing the body, and noticing how the mind checks, braces, and labels alarm. The emphasis is on learning to let feeling be felt without turning it into a verdict about whether life is going right.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Two Trajectories 06:09 Letting Go 13:25 Settling Into Practice 18:11 Undefended Presence 23:50 False Alarms 29:36 Softening the Heart
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/77006/

item.title: Moved by goodness: Reflections and Guided Metta Meditation (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
The talk starts with mindfulness as a kind of deconstruction: taking apart the tangle of selfing, fear, and clinging to see what is actually happening. From there it turns toward reconstruction through love, especially metta as a free, measureless love that is not bound up with control and that can be informed by grief without becoming naïve. The session then moves into a guided loving-kindness practice, using simple phrases and an invitation to sit as if one could do no wrong, with attention to the quiet reverberations of the words in the body.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Deconstruction and love 02:17 What love means here 05:28 The four divine abodes 09:43 Moved by goodness 12:35 Cultivation and purification 17:12 Settling into practice 25:15 Self-blessing phrases 31:40 Expansiveness of love
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/77000/

item.title: Guided Meditation_Not reading the tea leaves of the body
item.description:
The talk asks what happens if we stop trying to read a hidden message in the body and instead meet each sensation as an invitation to let go. It moves from the ways the mind builds a life story out of hope, fear, and prediction into a simpler practice of receiving bodily experience without turning it into a problem to solve. A brief reference to the Mula Sutta frames liberation as the core of Dharma, and the meditation returns again and again to the same quiet instruction: let go.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening into many worlds 04:41 Receiving the body 11:53 Autobiography and prediction 17:02 Safety and false alarms 22:06 Reading tea leaves 28:53 Liberation as the core 30:00 Doorways of perception 33:43 Renouncing words
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/17995

item.title: Guided Meditation: Not reading the tea leaves of the body
item.description:
What if the body is not giving clues to decode, but simply asking to be met? This guided meditation keeps returning to direct receiving: noticing sensation, affect, fatigue, pleasure, and the ways the mind turns them into a story about the next moment. Rather than solving the self’s riddle, the practice invites a softer response—treating alarm as possibly false, staying intimate with experience, and letting it all point toward release. A brief invocation of the Mula Sutta widens that frame, suggesting that every wall can become a doorway to Dharma when met without words.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening into welcome 02:43 Receiving the body 05:09 Not solving problems 07:16 Past and future pressure 14:38 False alarms 17:01 This moment only 23:31 Letting go repeatedly 28:53 Liberation in the sutta 33:44 Renouncing words
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/17989

item.title: Guided Meditation: Settling and Opening
item.description:
A simple instruction runs through this guided meditation: body in the posture, mind in the body, relaxation in the mind. From there, the talk keeps returning to the breath as a place to meet fatigue, agitation, and the habit of turning sensations into stories about whether things are okay. It also opens out into walking practice, showing how to carry mindfulness through movement without trying to fix anything or force a result.
Chapters: 00:00 Simple settling 02:00 Fear and interpretation 07:25 Breath as refuge 18:06 Questions dissolve 23:35 Cycles of breath 33:37 Retreat opens the heart 38:20 Meeting what arises 41:20 Walking with continuity 51:53 No conclusions today
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/17986

item.title: Guided Meditation: Stability and Change
item.description:
How do you rest when everything is already moving? This guided meditation begins by settling thought and feeling, then turns toward gravity, the earth’s support, and the breath as ways to steady the body and mind. From there, the talk opens into a direct contemplation of change: everything in motion, nothing really owned, a river with no banks. The practice keeps returning to a balance between stability and the changing conditions of life, including a brief reflection on how experience can feel open, permeable, and hard to protect.
Chapters: 00:41 Settling Together 02:11 Resting In Awareness 04:42 Gravity And Breath 08:41 Everything In Motion 10:27 Refuge And Center 13:12 Flexible Love 17:10 River Of Life 23:35 Open Mesh Of Being
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/17880

item.title: Guided Meditation: Sensitivity all the way down
item.description:
What if the heart-mind is not a thing to defend, but a system of receptivity—always being touched, always quivering, and hard to seal off? This guided meditation settles into that question by repeatedly returning to embodiment, sensing the pull to brace against pain or protect the self, and then softening into openness instead of conclusions. Rather than trying to get anywhere, the practice rests with the intensity of being sensitive all the way down, where the talk suggests there is already a kind of safety and goodness present.
Chapters: 00:00 Settling In 01:22 Chitta as Receptivity 03:22 Porous and Poised 05:28 Always Being Touched 10:00 False Alarms 16:40 Bracing and Protection 18:57 Openness Without Defense 24:50 Goodness Already Here
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/17755

item.title: Guided Meditation: Openness of Awareness
item.description:
What changes when attention stops rehearsing agitation and instead settles into breath, brightness, and release? The talk moves from a simple reminder that how we attend shapes experience into a guided practice of relaxing the body, welcoming the breath, and sensing the inhale and exhale as energizing, soothing, and eventually spacious. As the practice deepens, it shifts from a smaller, protected feeling to something like the openness of awareness, while also noting how rest can change the way hope, fear, and the loose ends of life are seen.
Chapters: 00:12 Opening Welcome 00:51 Attention Shapes Experience 01:50 Settling the Body 05:34 Breath as Practice 09:07 Release and Suspension 13:15 Seclusion in Breath 19:57 Hope and Fear 23:57 Closing Reflection
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/17753

item.title: Guided Meditation: Thought as the exhaust from engine of affect
item.description:
What if thought is only the exhaust from the engine of affect, and practice is learning not to believe every plume of smoke? The talk begins with simple attunement to the present, then moves into how bodily feeling gets read, interpreted, and quickly turned into stories about what we want, fear, or must change. From there, the instruction settles into a repeated refrain: soften—treat the urgency of feeling as a false alarm, rest with experience, and let the heart become less armored without overmaking meaning out of every surge and collapse.
Chapters: 00:12 Arriving Together 01:41 Attunement and Empathy 05:21 Feeling Becomes Meaning 08:55 Reading the Body 11:26 Not Over-Interpreting 14:04 Soften 20:29 Resting in Unpleasantness 23:01 Keeping Connection
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/17734

item.title: Guided Meditation: Entering the Dharma
item.description:
What happens if practice is less about fixing yourself and more about entering, simply entering, into the Dharma? This guided meditation begins with recollection and a tactile sense of being held, then settles into breath, awareness, and a posture of receptivity. Along the way, the talk returns to Thich Nhat Hanh’s image of nectar settling in a glass, using it to point toward surrender, acceptance, and a quieter trust in openness.
Chapters: 00:00 Settling In 01:40 Entering the Dharma 04:06 Nectar Settles 07:13 Recollecting Refuge 09:38 Breath and Blessing 11:04 Receptive Attention 16:30 Surrender and Acceptance 23:36 Trusting Openness
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/17743

item.title: Guided Meditation: Surrendering to the Present
item.description:
What does it mean to meet the present without trying to manage it? This guided meditation moves gently from settling the body and relaxing attention into an inquiry about clinging, hope, and control, with pauses to notice whatever wants awareness’s blessing so it can be put down. The practice keeps returning to embodiment and equanimity, suggesting that when we stop reaching to steer each moment, the heart becomes more available to receive what is here.
Chapters: 01:02 Arrival and settling 02:20 Relief and embodiment 05:04 Blessing what remains 09:53 Body and mind together 15:05 Clinging and loss 21:32 Less entanglement 23:34 Receiving the moment
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/17744

item.title: Guided Meditation: Letting the Winds of Feeling Blow
item.description:
How do you stay with feeling without immediately turning it into a story? The talk begins by settling the heart into a kind of Dharma posture, then moves into the idea that practice is a gradual sensitizing to goodness and to what is already wholesome in experience. From there it turns to the core instruction: let sensations, emotion, and impulse move through without over-interpreting them, trusting awareness and the quiet steadiness that comes from not needing to resolve everything at once.
Chapters: 00:00 Settling In 00:54 Dharma Posture 03:19 Moved by Goodness 05:36 Wisdom and Love 12:54 Feeling and Freedom 14:28 Letting Feeling Pass 17:13 Not Making Meaning 23:26 Surrender and Refuge
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/17632

item.title: Guided Meditation: Beginning with Goodness
item.description:
This guided meditation begins by turning attention toward goodness already present: in oneself, in others, in peace, love, wisdom, and in the ordinary ground of the path. From there, the practice settles into the body and breath, using simple reminders of goodness whenever the mind gets tight or contracted, so the breath can be received with more ease and less effort. The talk keeps returning to the felt rhythm of giving and receiving through breathing, and to the possibility of resting in care without having to manufacture anything special. It closes with a poetic refrain that you do not need to manufacture the sacred, underscoring the central move of recognizing what is already here.
Chapters: 01:24 Beginning with goodness 04:38 Settling in the body 10:54 Breath as nourishment 18:09 Relaxing vigilance 19:55 Devoting ourselves here 27:33 The sacred already present
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/17637
