item.title: Guided Meditation: Letting the Breath Come to You
item.description:
The talk starts with a simple invitation to sit, then turns quickly to a set of questions about what actually changes when meditation begins: what tightens, what loosens, and where attention goes to hide or rest. From there, the practice opens out from a narrow object of attention to the larger fact of awareness itself, with the breath treated as something received rather than controlled. The guidance keeps returning to letting the breath come to you, trusting what cannot be stopped, and resting in the openness that includes everything without making the rest of experience suspect.
Chapters: 00:12 Arriving Together 01:28 What Meditation Does 04:41 Resting In Openness 06:51 Open Awareness 11:38 Starting With Wise View 12:42 Breath As The Circle 15:51 Receiving The Breath 20:10 Nothing Outside Is Suspicious 21:24 Gentle Inclining
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/17638

item.title: Guided Meditation: Self and Space
item.description:
What happens if, instead of trying to get somewhere in meditation, you rest in what cannot be stopped? The talk moves from an opening gesture of simple aliveness into repeated instructions to let go of acquisitiveness, put down the notion of "my life," and rest in awareness itself. It also traces how the sense of self is repeatedly built from reference points, then points toward a more open way of perceiving that does not depend on those constructions.
Chapters: 00:00 Simple aliveness 02:57 Giving up acquisition 07:49 Rest where awareness stays 15:59 Putting down life 20:11 Hindrances and disenchantment 24:27 How self is reiterated 29:26 Rest in dependence 34:15 Love and surrender 42:07 Practice in daily life
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/17174

item.title: Guided Meditation: Affect and Motivation
item.description:
How do feeling and motivation shape what we think we know? The talk begins with a close, embodied attention to posture, breath, and the changing sensations of the body, then moves into how affective states arise as urges toward or away from experience. From there it turns to the way our stories, preferences, and sense of urgency can obscure the raw simplicity of feeling, and invites a practice of staying close, softening wanting, and trusting awareness more than narrative.
Chapters: 00:00 Settling into posture 02:30 Breath and relaxation 03:58 Body as sensation 08:51 Softening the heart 12:29 No preference for spaciousness 20:57 Affect and motivation 26:52 Knowing and letting go 35:31 Clinging and narrative 46:23 Practice rhythms and closing
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/17161

item.title: Guided Meditation: Attention & Awareness
item.description:
This guided meditation begins by noticing the shift from ordinary conversation into formal practice, then invites a very natural settling rather than forcing the mind into a preset mode. The talk moves through relaxing what can be relaxed, letting go of agitation, and making peace with the imperfect moment so attention can glide more smoothly over breath, body, and sound. It then opens into a quieter pivot: rather than continually directing the spotlight of attention, rest back in awareness itself, the knowing that cannot be stopped.
Chapters: 00:25 Arriving to meditate 02:22 Natural beginning 08:22 Relaxing and letting go 10:03 Attention settles 14:43 Less control 15:38 Resting in awareness 20:30 Spotlight and clinging 26:31 Unpartitioned knowing
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/17013

item.title: Guided Meditation: Sound, Outer and Inner
item.description:
A simple question runs through this guided meditation: what happens if sound is allowed to move through awareness instead of being held and chewed on? The practice begins by settling into the room, opening to the whole soundscape, and noticing even distant, faint, or ending sounds without preferring the loud or near ones. From there, the talk turns inward, inviting listeners to recognize thinking itself as an auditory event—just another sound, not a special category of self.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening and Settling 02:15 Beginning with Goodness 08:08 Outer Soundscape 14:27 Sound Passing Through 20:08 Hearing Inner Speech 25:45 Thoughts as Sound
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/17010

item.title: Guided Meditation: Faith in Awareness
item.description:
This guided meditation begins with posture and the simple task of meeting whatever is here, fresh and familiar at once. The practice turns toward the body’s memory of steadiness and the possibility of a dharma posture that does not depend on pleasant conditions or visible progress. Instead of looking for signs that meditation is “working,” the talk invites a quieter move: settle back, trust awareness, and let the seeds of awakening be present without being engineered.
Chapters: 00:12 Entering the sit 01:06 Familiar and fresh 03:17 Meeting this moment 06:23 Dharma posture 12:29 No need for signs 14:43 Faith and trust 21:32 Already enough 28:24 Listen and rest
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/17001

item.title: Guided Meditation: Unseduced by the Mirage of the Future
item.description:
What happens when meditation stops becoming a project? This guided practice keeps returning to a simple shift: relax the urge to prove something, soften the energy of self-improvement, and attend to breath, body, sound, and darkness without turning them into a test. Again and again, the talk settles into the sense that the ingredients of waking up are already present, even as the mind keeps reaching toward a future it tries to imagine and control.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening and settling 01:19 Natural beginning 04:49 No need to prove 07:06 Attending lightly 12:09 Time and projection 18:49 Unseduced by future 19:58 Enoughness and surrender 25:57 Opening to phenomena
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/16996

This guided practice keeps returning to a simple shift: relax the urge to prove something, soften the energy of self-improvement, and attend to breath, body, sound, and darkness without turning them into a test. Again and again, the talk settles into the sense that the ingredients of waking up are already present, even as the mind keeps reaching toward a future it tries to imagine and control.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening and settling 01:19 Natural beginning 04:49 No need to prove 07:06 Attending lightly 12:09 Time and projection 18:49 Unseduced by future 19:58 Enoughness and surrender 25:57 Opening to phenomena
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/16996

item.title: Guided Meditation
item.description:
This guided meditation begins by settling into a sustainable posture and then asking the listener to let the Dharma, muscle memory, and prior practice lead rather than the thinking mind. The attention is gently tilted toward whatever brings tranquility and ease — the breath, sensations in the hands or feet, the darkness behind closed eyes, even ambient noise — so the mind can take its cues from silence and soothing presence. From there, the talk rests in the idea that tranquility can help digest what is unfinished or tender in the heart, while also staying with the wholesomeness of intention and the wish to wake up. In the end, it returns to a simple gesture of awareness and letting time be an ally.
Chapters: 00:00 Settling In 00:48 Letting Practice Lead 02:18 Opening to Stillness 06:12 Finding Soothing Objects 08:19 Resting In Ease 12:41 Tranquility and Healing 14:43 Goodness of Intention 19:55 Time As Ally
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/16992

item.title: Guided Meditation
item.description:
The talk begins as a guided settling, with attention on posture, breath, and making space for the sounds and sensations of arriving. From there, it turns toward a subtle practice question: can the winds of experience—pleasant, unpleasant, grasping, aversion—be allowed to move through without bracing around a protected center of self? The guidance keeps returning to openness, not pretending anything is different, and to the sense that awareness can soften the body and heart as experience is simply met.
Chapters: 00:00 Settling In 02:23 Welcoming Experience 04:14 Breath and Ease 08:48 Letting Winds Blow 12:15 Grasping and Aversion 16:38 No Need To Pretend 22:49 Softening The Heart 27:49 Sustained Open Awareness
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/16985

item.title: Guided Meditation: Effort and Effortlessness
item.description:
How do effort and ease belong together in meditation? The talk guides a steady practice of landing in posture, choosing an anchor for attention, and then noticing that the work of directing attention can support a quieter, more effortless awareness. Rather than trying to make experience disappear, it points toward relaxing the grip on thought, resting in what cannot be stopped, and letting experiences arise in open space.
Chapters: 02:54 Landing and settling 06:26 Choosing an anchor 13:12 Effort and awareness 18:56 Relaxing the grip 20:17 Less mesmerized 25:01 Letting the face soften 27:38 Opening further
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/16824

item.title: Guided Meditation: Getting Somewhere by Going Nowhere
item.description:
Wanting something is one thing; getting it is another, and the talk begins by turning that familiar tension into a meditation question. It moves through the idea that practice is both goal-oriented and non-striving: we are headed toward more freedom, but we get there by relaxing the goal-making mind, softening grasping, and settling into one moment at a time. The guided portion returns again and again to awareness in the body, then opens into the sense that every sensation and thought can be received as an invitation to liberation rather than something to control.
Chapters: 00:27 Goals and Dukkha 01:56 Telic and Atelic 03:01 One Moment at a Time 05:29 Aspiration Without Urgency 08:38 Settling Into the Body 15:33 Liberation in Phenomena 17:58 Relaxing the Struggle 23:29 Be Blissful
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/16821

item.title: Guided Meditation: Meditation as an Atelic Activity
item.description:
What if meditation is not something to finish, improve, or use, but a way of resting with nowhere to go? The talk begins with a reflection on goal-directed living and the fatigue it can bring, then turns to the idea that a life organized entirely around achievement can become self-destructive. From there, the guided practice repeatedly releases striving, progress, and self-improvement, returning again and again to the breath, the body, sound, and the simple fact of being here.
Chapters: 00:55 Goals and Their Limits 02:22 Dukkha and Fatigue 03:02 Meditation Beyond Goals 04:21 Arriving in the Body 05:08 Settling and Surrendering 11:39 When Striving Returns 13:27 Breath as Reminder 17:27 Thoughts Beside the Point 24:29 Rest in Imperfection
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/16817

item.title: Guided Meditation: No Fidelity to the Past Moment
item.description:
What if the next moment does not have to obey the mood of the last one? The talk begins with a reading about grief, gratitude, anger, boredom, and happiness sharing the same stage, then turns toward the practice point: mindfulness does not try to keep one state going or force another to disappear. From there it stays close to the felt experience of sitting—landing, letting words melt away, breathing into the body, and noticing how intimacy can be lost in doing and doubt, then found again without narrativizing what meditation is becoming.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening Welcome 00:44 Reading on Mixed Feeling 03:52 No Fidelity to the Past 05:34 Settling Into Practice 10:48 Listening With Spaciousness 13:58 Freshness and Discontinuity 25:22 Moment-by-Moment Offering
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/16811

item.title: Guided Meditation: Landing in the Body Breathing
item.description:
Begin with the body breathing, then let awareness widen into the places where effort, worry, and the urge to fix life show up. The talk moves gently between settling with the breath, noticing how thinking is fueled by feeling, and bringing those charged places into patience and love rather than turning them into enemies. When the mind starts to gather fragments around plans and security, it keeps returning to the body as a steady reference point, with mindfulness presented as a quiet surrender of projects for the sake of awareness.
Chapters: 00:13 Opening and settling 02:10 Love as reference 05:13 Breath through the body 07:28 Gentle discipline 13:07 Meeting the urge to fix 16:06 Returning to breathing 21:23 Mindfulness as surrender
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/16813

item.title: Morning sit with instruction (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
How do attitude, attention, and awareness work together when practice starts to feel both deliberate and effortless? This guided morning sit moves from settling the body and establishing goodwill, to choosing different anchors for attention—the breath, the whole body, the space behind closed eyes, and the soundscape—while repeatedly returning to the sense that awareness does not need to be forced. The talk later broadens into brief Q&A on pain, eating, and walking, with a grounded emphasis on gentleness, imperfection, and finding what actually supports practice.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Opening and orientation 00:44 Three facets of practice 04:20 Softening the body 08:14 Breath and stability 19:14 Whole-body and sound 29:50 Resting in awareness 36:17 Questions on practice 43:56 Eating and wanting 53:34 Walking and closing
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/72188/

item.title: Guided Meditation
item.description:
How does attention settle without being forced? The talk opens as a guided sitting practice and uses the image of nectar sinking to the bottom of a glass to point to two modes of practice: a precise, effortful directing of attention and a softer opening that lets experience settle on its own. From there, the guidance turns toward letting everything remind you of the Dharma, forgiving thought, sensation, affect, and sound, and resting in an awareness that does not need to be propped up.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening and settling 02:49 Nectar image 03:13 Precise or open 04:49 Quiet guidance 08:22 Forgiving phenomena 11:05 Deepening rest 18:42 Effort and ease
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/16543

item.title: Tuesday Sit with Instructions: Affect and Meaning-making
item.description:
The talk starts by settling the body—softening the face, relaxing what can be relaxed, and noticing the buzzing, blooming field of sensation. From there it turns to a simple but pointed question: what happens when urgency, worry, and unpleasant feeling are treated as signals to interpret, rather than as feelings to be met directly? The speaker keeps returning to affect as a bodily, bidirectional event—something to meet with permission, patience, and awareness—so that thought and action are less driven by threat. By the end, the emphasis is on letting meaning-making rest for a while and meeting the moment without building a story around it.
Chapters: 00:00 Settling and Softening 02:21 Body as Attention 07:22 Worry and Threat 11:27 Affect and Awareness 15:39 Urgency and Equanimity 20:19 Feeling Without Story 28:15 Soft Heart, Open Body 36:18 Meaning in Its Place
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/16497

item.title: Guided Meditation - Grief and Compassion (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
Grief is woven into loving, so the practice here keeps returning to the same quiet question: how do we meet what keeps changing? The talk moves from settling into the body to reflections on impermanence, a Leonard Cohen lyric about sorrow that cannot be timed, and an image from Stephen Levine of traveling in fog with only headlights’ reach. From there, the guidance softens into repeated invitations to mercy, tenderness, and reverence for the grieving world, until sorrow is held as part of the heart’s life rather than something to get past.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:20 Settling In 01:27 Welcoming the Body 02:37 Love and Grief 05:40 Impermanence and Tenderness 11:10 Traveling in Fog 15:31 Holding Sorrow 19:10 Love Deepened 20:04 Tender Phrases
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/70749/

item.title: Guided Meditation on Body and Affect (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
Start with relaxing what can be relaxed, then widen that ease through the face, shoulders, belly, and the whole body. The talk moves from simple settling into breath awareness to a close look at affect as sensation in the body: the tugs, surges, alarm bells, and the way vigilance and clinging build a felt world of “something needs to happen.” It then opens into a broader instruction to meet experience with equanimity and radical permission, and later turns to how that same mindfulness can show up in everyday life, especially in the messier territory of eating, shame, and desire. The final part grounds the practice in continuity off the cushion—walking, daily activities, and the light awake attention that keeps noticing where the mind goes to make a home.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:01 Relaxing the body 05:14 Whole-body breath 10:31 Smoothing intensity 15:44 Affect and alarm 18:56 Permission to settle 22:02 Releasing grasping 26:32 Continuity beyond the cushion 41:33 Mindfulness and eating 53:31 Retreat instructions
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/70714/

item.title: Lightly Guided Meditation
item.description:
What happens if you stop trying to manage experience and simply let it all be here? This lightly guided meditation moves from settling into safety, to softening around vigilance and disconnection, and then to a gradual surrender of reference points like past and future, self and other, liking and disliking. A teacher’s instruction—“Don’t fight with yourself at any level”—becomes a pivot toward radical permission, non-resistance, and a more intimate resting with what is arising. By the end, the talk settles into equanimity: not picking sides, letting the heart rest, and noticing the natural dance of experience.
Chapters: 00:57 Opening to Experience 01:58 Safe Enough to Melt 02:42 Letting Armor Unwind 06:02 Dropping Reference Points 10:00 No Fighting 15:50 Tolerating Not Knowing 20:30 Resting the Heart
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/16271

item.title: Guided Meditation: Non-possessiveness and Joy
item.description:
What happens when letting go is no longer treated as loss, but as a way of becoming open to joy? The talk begins with the ordinary tangle of a world where “a million things” are right and wrong at once, then moves into resting the heart in the present rather than living in hope and fear. From there it turns toward non-possessiveness and mudita, showing how releasing comparison and clinging can make another person’s happiness feel unthreatening, even shared.
Chapters: 00:25 Settling In 00:55 What Is Here 02:38 Putting Down the World 03:38 Hope Into This Moment 08:26 Letting Go and Joy 11:08 Porous to Others 14:56 Wishing Happiness Well 20:43 No Self, Just World 24:26 Extending the Wish
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/16265

item.title: Guided Meditation: Compassion
item.description:
How does compassion move from feeling another's pain to resting in a warmth that does not exhaust the heart? This guided meditation begins with making room for the joys and sorrows of being human, then turns toward a person who is suffering, first taking in the reality of their pain and the willingness to grieve. From there, the practice shifts into offering love and blessings, letting go of the need to fix anything and settling into an open, distinction-less compassion.
Chapters: 00:12 Arriving and settling 01:25 Space for human life 05:24 Relaxing control 08:24 Blessing the moment 14:26 Seeing another's pain 18:39 From grief to warmth 24:01 Offering care 27:24 Open compassion
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/16258

item.title: Guided Meditation: Lovingkindness Practice
item.description:
This guided meditation begins by distinguishing metta from niceness: the practice is not about pretending to feel something you do not feel, but about meeting the heart honestly. From there, the talk turns toward the body as something already alive with care, even generosity, and invites a gentle settling into the signs of warmth, softening, and love that are already present. The core movement is simple and steady: silently repeat phrases like “may I be happy,” “may I be safe, protected,” and “may I live with ease,” while releasing the vigilance of self-patrolling, arrogance, and self-hatred.
Chapters: 01:08 Metta, Not Niceness 03:38 Kindness Toward the Body 06:40 Noticing Quiet Goodness 16:45 The Bell of Phrases 20:25 May I Be Happy 22:20 May I Be Safe 27:42 Unconditional Offering
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/16267

item.title: Lightly Guided Meditation
item.description:
The talk begins with a simple practice of settling into awareness, relaxing the body, and letting attention rest where it naturally cannot be stopped. From there, the speaker turns to a subtle distinction: while attention can be directed to breath, body, feeling, or sound, awareness itself is always already present and does not require effort to arise. A repeated invocation of Shantideva becomes part of that movement, helping fold the familiar ground of strategizing and fretting back into spacious awareness.
Chapters: 00:12 Settling In 03:01 Relaxing the Body 05:56 What Can't Be Stopped 07:48 Directing Attention 13:41 Suffering and Identification 16:04 Shantideva Refrain 21:57 Folding Life In 28:43 Closing Bells
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/16257

item.title: Lightly Guided Meditation
item.description:
The talk moves from settling the body and taking fuller breaths into a simple question of receptivity: can the heart become available to experience rather than brace against it? From there, the speaker connects softening and relaxing with seeing each moment as dharma, where acceptance deepens into gratitude and even pleasure and pain are held as gifts. The later movement turns toward judgment, self-worth, and self-indictment, loosening them into a larger field of presence in which innocence, care, and love feel increasingly natural.
Chapters: 00:30 Settling In 02:29 Becoming Available 06:47 Each Moment as Gift 11:23 Beyond Judgment 19:13 Basic Innocence 20:25 Releasing the Past 22:00 Awareness Holds It 24:56 Love Flavors Everything
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/16102

item.title: Lightly Guided Meditation
item.description:
The talk begins with settling the posture and breath, then quickly turns toward a lighter way of practicing: releasing tension, tolerating what remains, and recognizing that we are not responsible for manufacturing experience, insight, or concentration. From there it narrows toward a simple, intimate attention to what is here—breath, body, feeling, sound, even darkness behind closed eyes—so the moment can be known more directly and less interpreted. A recurring thread is the shift from trying to extract or accumulate something from practice to a receptive willingness to be softened by the moment, with trust in the path doing its own work.
Chapters: 00:20 Settling In 02:21 Soft Exhalation 04:35 Not Responsible for Everything 07:25 Sincerity and Trust 12:43 Simplifying Experience 20:46 Receptivity and Softening 29:13 Surrender in Awareness
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/16091

item.title: Lightly Guided Meditation
item.description:
How do we practice when the mind keeps pulling toward the wound, the plan, or the next thing to manage? This lightly guided meditation keeps returning to the simple act of stopping—opening to what is here, drawing a clear but flexible circle around the chosen focus, and resting with balanced energy. The talk frames attention as something like a gust of wind: neither braced against, nor held onto, and then widens into the sense that every experience can become a doorway when the agenda is put down.
Chapters: 00:13 Welcome and settling 00:58 Stopping with courage 04:09 Loose ends of life 06:12 Wind through awareness 08:49 Drawing the circle 11:00 Balanced resting 15:34 Everything as a door 23:25 Sentience and love
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/16079

item.title: Lightly Guided Meditation
item.description:
The talk begins with a simple, steady invitation to practice together: full, deep breaths, a softening exhale, and attention brought down into the belly. From there, the guidance keeps returning to the body’s “densities” or pockets of intensity, suggesting that the breath can smooth them out without trying to solve the rest of life. The emphasis is on patient care—softening the face, jaw, eyes, and belly, and letting thinking become less urgent as the body is met with attention and warmth.
Chapters: 00:25 Settling In 00:55 Full Breaths 02:42 Soften the Body 03:44 Breath Rhythm 04:50 Smooth the Energy 07:56 Just for Now 09:52 Long Silent Practice 14:55 Emotion and Thought 17:24 Lovingly Working With Density
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/16074

item.title: Lightly Guided Meditation
item.description:
What happens when attention stops trying to stand outside the body and instead lets the whole field be permeated by awareness? The talk moves gently from settling into posture and a lightly guided sit into imagery of the body as porous, grounded, and vibrating space, then broadens into the way present experience can soften fixation and preoccupation with control. Near the end, it turns toward a quiet confidence in letting go: not as a task, but as a relief that does not solve everything, yet eases suffering in the moment.
Chapters: 00:00 Welcome and format 00:56 Settling the posture 02:44 Awareness in the body 05:00 Earth and space 08:43 Control and time 12:55 Letting the sit be 16:31 Softening through imperfection 23:19 Confidence in letting go
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/16076

item.title: Lightly Guided Meditation
item.description:
What does it mean to stop sorting the moment into pleasant and unpleasant, and simply let everything be part of arriving? The talk begins as a lightly guided meditation, using ambient sound and the breath to settle into the whole body and then into a softer relation with whatever is present. It moves into the idea that samsara keeps offering ways to optimize experience, while practice asks for a willingness to be present without defense against imperfection. The closing stretches into long, quiet repetitions that give the meditation room to deepen and loosen further.
Chapters: 00:41 Opening the sit 02:00 Ambient sound and settling 02:49 All phenomena as gateway 05:30 Breath and whole body 08:54 Not optimizing the moment 14:01 Undefended presence 20:10 Extended absorption 27:28 Quiet closing stretch
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/16051

item.title: Guided compassion practice: grieving as a factor of growth
item.description:
Grief here is treated not as a detour from compassion, but as one of the ways compassion deepens. The talk moves from settling into the body and sensing shared practice, into a clear teaching that letting go often includes loss: grief over impermanence, over the instability of the self, and over the suffering of the world. From there, it becomes a guided compassion practice—taking in another’s suffering, offering repeated wishes for relief, and exploring how care can feel painful while still becoming something generative and whole.
Location: Insight Meditation Society - Retreat Center
Chapters: 00:30 Settling In 02:01 Growth and Grief 07:09 Waking Up to Loss 09:25 Dharma and Generativity 11:36 Compassion as Action 14:44 Taking in Suffering 20:27 Grief and Karuna 26:05 Heart Practice 34:17 Boundless Well-Wishing
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/69766/

item.title: Guided meditation: feeling as a prediction of risk
item.description:
What happens when the body’s feeling of urgency is treated not as a command, but as a prediction of risk? The guided practice begins with natural breathing, softening bracing, and letting attention rest in different anchors before widening into sound, body, and background, with an emphasis on equanimity rather than fixing experience. Later, the talk turns to how the mind scans for threat and opportunity, how pain and aversion generate their own urgency, and how meeting that whole field with steadiness can reveal a quieter kind of freedom.
Location: Insight Meditation Society - Retreat Center
Chapters: 00:00 Settling the body 04:09 Finding an anchor 10:00 Object and background 17:05 Feeling as threat 24:13 False alarm 27:42 Opening to feeling 35:10 Pain and aversion 40:49 Walking and equanimity 55:14 Closing retreat instructions
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/69754/

item.title: Guided Metta practice
item.description:
What if metta is less a mood to produce than a way of stepping out of measuring self and other? The talk begins by laying out loving-kindness as cultivation, concentration, and purification, then moves into a guided practice that starts with the state of the heart, uses phrases like “May I be happy” and “May all beings be safe,” and returns again and again to whatever is actually present. Along the way, frustration, numbness, grief, and undigested pain are treated not as failures but as part of what the practice can reveal and include.
Location: Insight Meditation Society - Retreat Center
Chapters: 00:00 Why Metta Matters 04:53 Three Functions 09:13 Settling the Body 13:41 Self-Compassion Phrases 19:48 Extending to Others 24:53 Warmth and Care 30:01 Pain as Practice 34:01 All Beings at Ease 39:01 Closing
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/69738/

item.title: Guided Meditation: Letting Go Cultivates Stability
item.description:
What happens when settling the breath is approached less as concentration and more as letting go? The talk begins with a gentle body scan and a soft gathering of attention, then turns toward the idea that clinging keeps the mind busy and that simple awareness can make the rest of experience feel less urgent. From there it moves into an extended lovingkindness-style repetition, using the refrain "May you be blissful" to steady practice in non-optimization and acceptance of imperfection.
Chapters: 00:12 Settling In 05:24 Breath as Home 11:21 Letting Go of Better 17:21 Truce with Imperfection 23:40 Loving-Kindness Phrases 29:10 Trusting Awareness
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/14661

item.title: Guided Meditation: Don't Let Greed And Aversion Lurk Behind the Technique
item.description:
The talk begins with a careful question: when does reaching for meditation technique become another way of avoiding what is already here? The speaker traces a subtle tension between technique as a genuine support and technique as a defense against greed, aversion, clinging, or the mind’s wildness. From there, the guidance moves into a precise breath practice, asking for steady attention, courage, and a kind of peace with whatever feeling and preoccupation arise in the background.
Chapters: 00:45 Technique and avoidance 02:14 A subtle warning 04:00 Settling the body 06:29 Precise attention 11:28 When attention wanders 18:42 Shelter and release
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/14568

item.title: Guided Meditation: Not Reiterating or Reimaging Self
item.description:
What happens if, for one sitting, you stop trying to solve your life, improve the moment, or figure out what it is for? This guided meditation repeatedly returns to resting with the breath, then gently notices how the mind keeps re-imagining the body, the room, and the story of “me.” The practice movement is to soften that subtle self-making and let awareness hold even the urgent energies, rather than feeding them with more narrative.
Chapters: 00:37 Settling In 01:54 Awareness First 05:21 Resting Without Agenda 09:52 Softening Self-Image 12:46 Autobiography in Awareness 18:28 Meeting Urgency 20:09 Space Moving Through 27:12 Nothing To Figure Out
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/14570

item.title: Guided Meditation: Clinging Complicates Everything
item.description:
The talk begins by settling the body and breath: if something feels overactive, numb, or heavy, the practice is to respond with the right kind of attention and breathing until the whole field feels more unified. From there, the focus turns to clinging as a kind of inner whirlpool—something agitating and complicating that shows up as greed, aversion, and habit. The movement of the meditation is simple and repetitive: soften what is tense, enliven what is dull, and practice non-clinging until the moment itself feels less crowded and more open.
Chapters: 00:12 Settling In 02:54 Balancing Energy 05:50 Breath Through the Body 09:27 The Cost of Clinging 12:15 Equanimity and Ease 14:13 Resting in Simplicity 22:12 Sacred Enough
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/14560

item.title: Guided Meditation: Sound and the Openness of Awareness
item.description:
The practice begins with settling the body—softening the face, throat, shoulders, and belly—while noticing how stillness can feel like entering water whose temperature is unknown. From there, the attention is gently turned toward the soundscape: beginnings and endings of sound, the loud and the faint, the sudden and the steady, as a way to notice how vigilance narrows experience. The talk then points to a different mode altogether, where awareness can rest back receptively, with sound becoming a gateway into something expansive and effortless.
Chapters: 01:02 Settling In 02:58 Softening the Body 08:02 Alert and Soft 10:15 Entering Soundscape 13:58 Sound Fading 18:03 Resting in Awareness
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/14558

item.title: Guided Meditation: Grounding and Opening
item.description:
Start with the body as it is, then let the breath and gravity help it settle. This guided meditation moves from grounding into a wider, more open awareness, with repeated gentle cues to soften, relax, and notice that knowing is already happening. As the practice unfolds, the talk returns again and again to imperfection as part of the path, suggesting that what is unfinished or unsettled can still become a vehicle for peace.
Chapters: 00:12 Opening Context 02:57 Settling Into Body 05:47 Awareness and Softening 10:02 Holding Imperfection 12:23 Returning to Ground 18:43 Letting Dharma Unfold
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/14556

item.title: Guided Meditation: Resting in Goodness
item.description:
This guided meditation begins by asking you to notice the body as it remembers practice: the letting go, patience, equanimity, stillness, and determination already present in your life. From there, the talk widens into gratitude for the Dharma lineage and the blessings of having heard the call toward freedom, then turns into a simple practice of opening to sound, silence, and the whole soundscape as one field of attention. In the closing, the emphasis softens into resting the heart itself—relaxing the grip of clinging and allowing the heart some needed rest.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening and settling 01:21 Remembering goodness 02:46 Lineage and gratitude 04:42 Resting in blessing 12:36 Sound as one field 13:47 Deepening into ease 24:47 Letting the heart rest
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/14008

item.title: Guided Meditation: Being Changed by Experience Rather than Changing it
item.description:
This guided meditation begins with settling the body and finding a posture that is supple but strong, then rests attention in a quiet openness to sights, sounds, sensations, and thought. The practice repeatedly turns away from pushing and pulling on experience, asking instead for a softer heart and a willingness to be impacted. As the talk unfolds, it names the tension between trying to shape life and letting experience do its work, ending by encouraging a steady, receptive way of feeling one's way through the flood of urges.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening and arrival 01:15 Finding the posture 03:53 Releasing tension 06:54 Softening toward experience 13:27 Feeling at the center 15:27 Willing to be changed 18:38 Standing down 20:29 Closing turn
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/13999

item.title: Guided Meditation: Softening to the Ungovernability of Experience
item.description:
How do you stay open when experience feels ungovernable—like stepping into water that is too cold? This guided meditation invites the listener to let the body settle, feel the downward pull of gravity, and soften around the flood of dukkha without trying to sort every part of life into right and wrong. The practice moves toward equanimity not as distance, but as a way the heart can bow to reality and become gentler within it.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening Reflection 02:24 Settling Into Stillness 05:43 Body and Gravity 08:05 Not Sorting It Out 11:05 Freedom In Experience 16:13 Bowing To Dukkha 17:06 Silent Practice 30:15 Closing Bells
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/13994

item.title: Guided Meditation: Between Suppression and Entanglement
item.description:
How do you relax enough to open, without falling into either self-suppression or getting caught in the story of yourself? The talk moves gently through posture, softening the face, shoulders, belly, and hands, then broadens into resting in the presence and goodwill of Sangha, Buddha, and Dharma. From there it turns toward a quieter practice of blessing experience with awareness, letting solidity thin into space and making peace with the moment. It ends by naming the middle way the practice is feeling toward: somewhere between suppression and entanglement.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening Welcome 00:39 Sustainable Posture 02:02 Presence and Softening 04:26 Relaxing the Body 08:08 Undefended Refuge 12:09 Blessing Experience 19:35 Releasing Strife 25:41 Middle Way Closing
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/13989
item.itunes:author: Matthew Brensilver
item.itunes:summary:
How do you relax enough to open, without falling into either self-suppression or getting caught in the story of yourself? The talk moves gently through posture, softening the face, shoulders, belly, and hands, then broadens into resting in the presence and goodwill of Sangha, Buddha, and Dharma. From there it turns toward a quieter practice of blessing experience with awareness, letting solidity thin into space and making peace with the moment. It ends by naming the middle way the practice is feeling toward: somewhere between suppression and entanglement.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening Welcome 00:39 Sustainable Posture 02:02 Presence and Softening 04:26 Relaxing the Body 08:08 Undefended Refuge 12:09 Blessing Experience 19:35 Releasing Strife 25:41 Middle Way Closing
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/13989

item.title: Guided Meditation: Not Fighting with Ourselves
item.description:
How can practice become a kind of nonviolence toward experience, instead of a constant inner struggle? The talk introduces equanimity through a simple meditation frame: letting sensory experience arise without interference, “not fighting with ourselves,” and relaxing the body and mind enough to stop treating ache and contraction as a collision. From there, it returns again and again to the same movement—softening awareness like space, giving phenomena permission to come and go, and seeing that this calm is not numbness but a deeper responsiveness to suffering and joy.
Chapters: 00:12 Welcome and frame 01:26 Equanimity theme 02:05 Non-interference 03:03 Sustainable posture 05:47 Practice for all 09:22 Softening around collision 11:44 Renouncing grasping 20:18 Letting go in practice 28:04 Equanimity deepens life
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/13982

item.title: Guided Meditation - changing conditions
item.description:
How do you stay with silence, hope, fear, and the felt fact that everything is changing? This guided meditation begins with settling the body and gently noticing the connotations of silence, then opens into emotional weather and the “landscape of hope and fear” without forcing an inner posture. From there it turns toward dukkha and anicca: the uneasy habit of wrestling with imperfection, the release of the illusion of permanence, and the sense that we are already in the river of change rather than standing outside it. The practice closes by softening the story of “me” and pointing toward a more radical openness and love.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:01 Settling In 01:38 Silence and Temperature 02:54 Hope and Fear 06:25 Body and Sensitivity 10:16 Opening to Dukkha 13:35 The Weight of Change 21:20 Anicca in Experience 29:25 River Without Banks 38:32 Letting Go to Love
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/68413/

item.title: Reflections and Guided Heart Practice
item.description:
This talk begins with a simple invitation to slow down together and practice receiving kindness from one another, then widens into a reflection on the Brahma-viharas as heart practices. The speaker traces loving-kindness as a kind of freedom: not a promise that life will feel pleasant all the time, but a tender, responsive heart that can receive love, soften, and begin again when the mind wanders. A short poem by Raymond Carver deepens the theme, pointing toward the wish to feel oneself beloved on the earth before the guided meditation moves from receiving to gently offering metta outward.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:01 Opening the Heart Practice 00:33 Community and Receiving 05:06 Kindness Over Attention 09:01 Brahma-viharas Explained 11:35 Freedom and Tenderness 13:51 Raymond Carver's Question 16:29 Metta as Free Heart 20:03 Settling and Imagining 24:31 Receiving Kindness
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/68409/

item.title: Guided Meditation
item.description:
What happens when meditation is stripped back to something very simple: just sitting, breathing, and meeting whatever the mind does next? The talk moves from a short reflection on practice as rest, release, and unification of attention into a long guided sit, then into walking practice as a way to keep awareness continuous through the day. Along the way, it keeps returning to the tension between striving and simply showing up, and to the idea that boredom, distraction, and even restlessness can be met without turning them into a problem.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Practice as Simplicity 06:58 Settling In 08:25 Body and Breath 17:21 Relaxation and Release 24:36 Returning Again 35:50 Offering the Heart 40:30 Rest and Intensity 42:10 Walking and Continuity
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/68404/

item.title: Friday Morning Instructions: The non-compulsion of awareness
item.description:
The talk begins with settling into posture in a way that feels welcoming, then quickly moves into a quiet inquiry: what if awareness does not compel us, even though body and mind are full of habit, memory, and urge? From there, the speaker traces how the present moment carries the past, how clinging shows up as tension and drivenness in the body, and how practice can become a gentle act of digesting life rather than sorting it into right and wrong. Later, the teaching turns toward liberation as something that frees experience itself, and closes by treating Dharma as a collection of skillful means that has to be felt out moment by moment.
Chapters: 00:30 Settling In 03:40 Breath and Body 07:53 The Past in the Present 15:31 Awareness Without Compulsion 24:21 Clinging in the Body 34:15 Freeing Experience 40:24 Putting Down Judgment 48:34 Skillful Means
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/12561

item.title: Wednesday Morning Instructions: Clarifying Body
item.description:
How do you stop fighting the body long enough to see it clearly? The talk begins by settling into the noise and silence around and within experience, then moves into a sustained body scan that emphasizes gravity, breath, and the shifting field of sensation. From there, it turns to clarifying body experience with higher resolution, calling a truce with imperfection, and resting in equanimity rather than trying to fix or maximize the moment.
Chapters: 00:00 Settling Into Silence 03:59 Gravity and Exhalation 09:07 Whole-Body Awareness 11:44 Unifying Attention 15:14 Clarifying Body Experience 26:05 Truce With Imperfection 36:14 Dharma Mind 40:25 Protecting Nothing 48:14 Carrying Practice Forward
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/12545

item.title: Monday Morning instructions
item.description:
What happens if meditation is less a way to achieve a special state and more a way to keep meeting experience as it unfolds? The talk moves from a broad view of practice—rest, refuge, clarity, renunciation, freedom—to very concrete instructions for sitting and walking: soften the face and belly, feel the body, anchor attention in the breath or the feet, and notice the pull toward clinging or stimulation. Near the end, the emphasis settles on continuity: not judging one moment, but letting another moment of mindfulness lead into the next.
Chapters: 00:00 What meditation is 06:33 Technique and trust 07:28 Settling the body 12:14 Truce with effort 22:18 Awareness of sensation 37:29 Breath and basic aliveness 44:01 Continuity in walking 51:02 Another moment of mindfulness
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/12526

item.title: Guided Meditation: Solid, Liquid, Gas
item.description:
The talk opens by asking what it means to stop trying to improve the moment and instead meet experience as it is. From there it turns into a guided meditation on how life moves between solid, liquid, and gas: suffering and craving harden things, mindfulness softens them, and repeated attention to anicca points back to change itself. Near the end, the teaching widens into compassion, including a brief story about a mother’s tender response to childhood hurt.
Chapters: 00:00 Arriving Without Agenda 02:23 Putting Down the Story 05:30 Allowing What Remains 11:13 Solid, Liquid, Gas 16:25 Anicca in the Body 27:12 Nothing to Hold 36:57 Compassion for the Heavy 38:51 Closing Bells
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/12263

item.title: Guided Meditation: Radical Permission to Arrive
item.description:
How do you settle into practice when nothing is meant to be excluded? The talk begins with orienting to the screen, the room, and the body, then moves into a clear invitation to make space for breath, sound, thought, and feeling without resistance. From there it opens into a guided sitting and walking practice: resting attention, allowing what is present to be present, and using movement to sustain continuity rather than treating walking as a break from practice.
Chapters: 00:01 Arrive in Space 02:26 Nothing Excluded 05:21 Permission to Be Here 11:50 Radical Self-Acceptance 21:22 Breath Trains the Mind 40:21 Gently with the First Day 47:24 Walking as Continuity
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/12257

item.title: Guided Reflection & Meditation: Grief & Compassion
item.description:
The talk begins by insisting that love and metta are not weakened by suffering; they become compassion when they meet it. From there it moves into a clear argument that grief is not a failure of practice but part of maturation, because a heart that can hold dukkha without hardening is a stronger and more ethical heart. The middle opens into a brief guided reflection on wishing safety and freedom for all beings, then widens again through a poem by Hala Alyan that gives image and language to impermanence, loss, and the tide of things being remade.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Opening Into Practice 01:36 Metta Meets Suffering 03:02 Groundlessness and Grief 05:15 Why We Grieve 08:44 Softening the Heart 13:42 Generative Grief Practice 22:38 Reading Hala Alyan 27:46 Letting Go Freely
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/63042/

item.title: Guided Meditation: Feelings and their elaborations
item.description:
What happens when feeling tone is noticed before it becomes story? This guided meditation keeps returning to the simple but easily missed field of experience—breath, posture, sensation, and the shifting pull of pleasant, unpleasant, and neutral—and shows how quickly those tones can harden into aversion, grasping, or confusion. The practice is steady and patient: rest with the body, sense the ebb and flow of feeling, and see what changes when experience is known directly rather than over-interpreted.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Arriving 01:58 Attention as care 08:33 Body first 17:42 Feeling tone 24:32 From sensation to story 36:35 Not building on feeling 39:30 Rumi and transition 49:38 Damasio and closing
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/63036/

item.title: Guided Meditation: Settling into the Body
item.description:
How do you settle when the mind is full of worry, craving, and restlessness, without feeling that you are neglecting what matters? The talk moves gently from that tension into a guided practice of resting attention in the body, breath, and posture, with repeated reminders that it is okay to rest and let the breath balance what is agitated. As the practice unfolds, the speaker emphasizes gravity, groundedness, and a simple trust in the natural rhythm of breathing rather than trying to perform meditation.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Opening Instructions 01:37 Taking Cues From Body 04:01 It Is Okay To Rest 05:10 Shared Intention 08:03 Gravity And Release 11:20 Natural Breathing 17:30 Porous To Breath 27:58 Innocent Mind Movements 29:58 Trusting The Dharma
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/63029/

item.title: Day 6 Morning Guided Instructions: Pervasiveness of Feeling (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
What happens when feeling, rather than the story about life, becomes the main doorway of practice? This guided morning talk starts with settling the body—breathing, grounding, surrendering to gravity—and then turns toward the felt texture of pleasant, unpleasant, and neutral experience, asking repeated “What is this?” to loosen habitual reactions. It draws on Ajahn Chah’s instruction to Ajahn Sumedho that love, hate, greed, and aversion can become aids rather than obstacles, and later brings in Antonio Damasio to underscore how thoroughly feeling accompanies mind and perception. The talk keeps returning to a simple but difficult movement: stay, investigate, and let awareness meet whatever arises without friction.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Choosing Awareness 02:52 Taking The Seat 08:10 Why Investigate 12:04 The Power Of Feeling 15:04 Ajahn Chah's Instruction 21:23 Finding Feelings In The Body 26:58 Thought And Cascade 34:07 Blooming And Collapsing 38:57 Radical Permission
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/59145/

item.title: Day 2 Morning Guided Instructions (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
Rest is available even amid the moment’s imperfection, and the talk keeps returning to how to let the body and breath make that rest real. It moves from a gentle scan for bracing and fear, to settling into gravity, exhalation, and the feeling that the whole body is breathing out, then toward a steadier attention that can release self-judgment and the usual reference points of past, future, good, and bad. Near the end, the speaker broadens into trust in the logic of practice, briefly citing Sharon Salzberg to underline that we do not need to understand the whole path in advance; sincerity is enough for the learning to unfold.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 01:01 Rest Is Available 04:11 Scanning for Bracing 09:17 Settling With Gravity 13:58 Breath in the Body 20:38 Sustaining Attention 25:13 Letting Go of Sorting 32:45 Falling Into Awareness 38:20 Trust and Practice Logistics
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/59015/

item.title: Morning Instructions
item.description:
This talk begins with the simple claim that this life is only this life, and that honoring it means paying attention. From there, the speaker moves into a practice of meeting the body without holding on: scanning for greed, ill-will, restlessness, dullness, doubt, and then seeing them clearly, as the Buddha does with Mara, without adding struggle. The teaching widens into conditionality—seeing cause and effect more deeply, until tenderness and love feel increasingly natural and self-harshness has less room. It closes by returning to a careful, agenda-free curiosity, with attention drawn back to sensation itself rather than to an image of the body.
Chapters: 00:00 This Life, Attended To 02:27 Meeting Hindrance 04:39 Directness Over Struggle 07:53 Seeing Conditionality 10:48 Body As Flow 14:27 Image And Sensation 18:17 No Hidden Agenda
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/10634

item.title: Guided Meditation: Body and Space (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
The meditation begins with a very simple anchor: breath, breath, breath, until the body settles enough to notice where effort has been tight or artificial. From there, the practice opens into a softer honesty—relaxing bracing, letting the present moment be met without defense, and then widening attention from the breath to the whole body and the surrounding soundscape. In the later part of the guided sit, the attention moves to the edge between body and space, where sensations, pain, heartbreak, and love are all felt as changing process rather than fixed things.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:01 Settling with breath 06:50 Relaxing effort 10:04 Simple presence 20:26 Letting go again 25:43 Body and space 32:41 Everything is process 38:40 Closing
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/57123/

item.title: Guided Meditation: How the world impacts the emotional body
item.description:
What happens when sounds, memories, and even a passing thought land in the body as feeling before they become story? This guided meditation begins by distinguishing physical sensation from emotional responsivity, then moves into breathing, the hands, and a sustained turning toward whatever the emotional body is already doing. The practice is steady and gentle, with long stretches of silence and repeated invitations to notice agitation, grief, restlessness, or ease without trying to fix them.
Location: Vallecitos Mountain Retreat Center
Chapters: 00:00 Opening reflection 02:46 Clinging and the body 06:48 Making peace with feeling 11:38 Mindfulness as refuge 13:48 Breath and hands 18:29 Emotional circuits 24:06 Permission and contact 29:08 Extended stillness 40:18 Grief and daily life
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/56694/

What happens when sounds, memories, and even a passing thought land in the body as feeling before they become story? This guided meditation begins by distinguishing physical sensation from emotional responsivity, then moves into breathing, the hands, and a sustained turning toward whatever the emotional body is already doing. The practice is steady and gentle, with long stretches of silence and repeated invitations to notice agitation, grief, restlessness, or ease without trying to fix them.
Location: Vallecitos Mountain Retreat Center
Chapters: 00:00 Opening reflection 02:46 Clinging and the body 06:48 Making peace with feeling 11:38 Mindfulness as refuge 13:48 Breath and hands 18:29 Emotional circuits 24:06 Permission and contact 29:08 Extended stillness 40:18 Grief and daily life
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/56694/

item.title: Guided Meditation: Body Settling, Pleasure of the Breath
item.description:
Fear and uncertainty are named right at the start, and the meditation answers them by inviting a simple spirit of love that can hold whatever is present. From there the talk settles into posture, body awareness, and breath, moving slowly from warming the whole body with attention to resting on the subtle pleasure of breathing in and out. Near the end, the speaker points to the mind’s habit of bracing and gently suggests relaxing even after the bell, noticing where unease still lingers.
Location: Vallecitos Mountain Retreat Center
Chapters: 00:00 Meeting Fear with Love 03:28 Posture and Grounding 04:42 Warming the Body 09:08 Finding the Breath 11:40 Sustaining Contact 14:21 Pleasure in Breathing 20:58 Thought and Letting Go 22:44 Simple Return 29:11 After the Bell
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/56638/

item.title: Guided Meditation: The Ground of Loving Acceptance
item.description:
Stopping the mind’s busyness is framed here as an impossible self-project: the self keeps searching for ground and cannot quite rest in itself. The guided practice turns instead toward breathing, sensing the emotional body, and meeting feeling with loving acceptance so clinging can soften rather than harden into suffering. As the talk unfolds, it repeatedly returns to the question of how little effort is needed to stay connected, and how to receive experience without trying to fix or curate it.
Chapters: 00:01 Selfing and strain 02:11 Loving acceptance begins 03:05 Finding the ground 06:28 No pressure to be someone 14:14 Feeling tone and clinging 18:39 Breathing into feeling 25:17 Softening the body 34:55 Ease and closing
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/10078

item.title: Guided Meditation: Settling through Love, Deepening through Sound
item.description:
What would life be without love, and how does a sit begin from that question? The talk moves from softening at the heart center into a sound practice that treats sounds, silence, and even thought as part of one field, with the invitation to listen without forcing anything. It gradually turns toward refuge: noticing how the mind keeps returning to the story of me, and seeing that pattern with enough continuity to rest in awareness instead. A brief Rumi passage near the end sharpens the emphasis on wholehearted practice, before the talk settles into a gentle, sustainable close.
Chapters: 00:00 Arriving Through Love 01:42 Softening the Heart 09:18 Kindness Without Pretense 13:36 Opening to Sound 27:18 Sound as One Field 38:32 Rumi on Wholeness 42:17 Refuge and Self-Story 46:05 Resting Sustainably
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/10073

item.title: Guided Meditation: Settling into the Pleasure of the Breath
item.description:
Starting with the feel of the whole body, this guided meditation moves by way of breath, posture, and a very gentle sense of ease. It invites attention to the subtle pleasure in different parts of the breath cycle, then uses that contact to signal safety to the mind and soften the heart. Later, the practice widens into walking meditation, where the talk explores continuity of awareness, meeting boredom, and keeping contact with simple sensations without getting tight.
Chapters: 00:00 Settling the body 02:36 Pleasure in the breath 07:10 Letting awareness flood 11:32 Safety and softening 19:27 Whole-body presence 29:35 Knowing the breath 33:18 Walking as continuity 40:29 Working with boredom 45:00 Practical retreat notes
Source: AudioDharma

item.title: guided meditation
item.description:
How does the mind come to rest when practice first feels overstimulating? The talk moves through posture, breath, and attention with practical adjustments: sometimes a clean reset is helpful, other times the mind needs a gentler unfurling into steadiness. From there, it explores how to work with fatigue, restlessness, and the habit of getting lost in thought, returning again and again to a wider sense of the body and the line between focus and distraction. In the last part, the emphasis turns to scanning for small tensions, relaxing what can be relaxed, and seeing how safety and settling allow the sense of self to thin out.
Location: New York Insight Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Settling the posture 01:28 Entering practice gently 05:36 Root and support 08:16 Breath and energy 11:53 Making peace with imperfection 19:04 This life, not rehearsal 23:36 Choosing the field of attention 27:40 Steadying the inner gaze 32:57 Scanning for clinging
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/55368/

item.title: Sitting with Instructions (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
What does it mean to let a retreat actually enter the heart, rather than just collect insights? The talk moves from the basic instruction to stay with sights, sounds, and the movement of mind into a larger emphasis on digestion, integration, and a freedom that is quiet rather than dramatic. Along the way, it turns toward love as the measure of awakening, then closes with questions about dreams, tears, and how to carry a retreat home without forcing closure.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 05:01 Experience as life 08:57 Digesting the day 14:35 Love as measure 25:33 Awakening into love 34:27 Subtle freedom 38:51 Endings and change 43:12 Dreams and letting go 48:08 Tears and integration
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/53260/

item.title: Day 6: Sitting with Instructions (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
How do you sit with urgency without pretending it isn’t there? The talk moves from a simple instruction to scan body and mind, through the repeated reminder that this moment, imperfect as it is, is good enough, and into a deeper inquiry into change, clinging, and the habit of freezing experience. From there it opens into a softer practice of offering the attention up rather than extracting something from meditation, then closes with metta practice and a brief reflection on keeping continuity of awareness in daily life.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 01:16 Starting the sit 01:51 Not pretending 07:31 Drawing the boundary 15:24 Urge and tension 23:25 No banks on this river 32:44 Offering experience 37:43 Turning to mettā 48:16 Continuity off the cushion
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/53239/

item.title: Guided Meditation: Turbulence and Peace (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
What if sadness, laughter, and peace can all be present at once? The talk opens with a story from a dark breathing group, where grief in one person evokes tears, then a kind of wisdom-laughter, and then a deeper stillness underneath both. From there it moves into a guided exploration of noting experience, the pull of craving and clinging, and the way retreat intensity can become material for learning rather than something to escape. Later questions turn to secular mindfulness, renunciation, and the uneasy territory of free will, with a quiet emphasis on trying without pretending certainty.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Grief and Peace 09:55 Noting Experience 16:08 Craving and Selfing 24:20 Letting Go 34:01 Retreat Energy 40:53 Mindfulness and Renunciation 49:39 Agency and Trying
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50735/

item.title: Guided Meditation: Clinging and Space (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
What happens when a mind braced against boredom, discomfort, or the feeling that something is missing learns to rest instead? The talk moves from settling the body and stabilizing attention to a close inquiry into clinging, craving, aversion, and the quiet ways dissatisfaction hardens into suffering. From there it opens into a gentler discipline: letting experience be as it is, softening the heart, and discovering that peace with imperfection can support clearer action rather than passivity.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Settling In 05:11 Finding the Anchor 12:35 Letting Go 18:29 Craving and Aversion 29:02 More Space Than Expected 39:58 Body and Thought 46:18 Nonviolence and Love 52:37 Silence and Integration
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50674/

item.title: Talk: Guided Meditation: Insight Into Not-self
item.description:
How do you investigate not-self without turning it into another fixed view? The talk moves from the way we freeze other people into concepts, to the way we freeze ourselves into a story that feels solid, defended, and hard to question. It then turns into a guided inquiry: noticing selfing in moment-to-moment experience, seeing how clinging and fear build a sense of center, and letting that center soften into more space, care, and ease. The closing movement is quiet and direct: as identification loosens, love and service become less effortful, more natural.
Chapters: 00:00 Seeing Beyond Concepts 03:36 Noting and Self Assumptions 09:09 Not-Self as Practice 13:39 Ego, Shame, and Comparison 17:59 Curating the Self 24:57 Thought and the Sense of I 32:29 Narrative Gravity and Fear 41:37 Open Air of Change 46:53 Not-Self and Love
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/8750

item.title: Guided Meditation: Letting Go of Becoming
item.description:
What happens if, for a moment, you stop trying to become someone and simply meet what is here? The guided meditation moves from repeated questions about stopping and beginning into a steadier invitation to rest in safety, simplicity, space, and silence. From there, the talk turns toward letting go of clinging to the next moment, the future, and even the need to resolve suffering in any final way. It ends with a quieter, more paradoxical note: that suffering matters, and also does not, and that love is what remains.
Chapters: 00:00 Entering Stillness 03:57 Safety and Simplicity 06:47 Awakening as Letting Go 13:06 Space Through the Mind 18:27 The Heart's Hole 24:24 Love After Letting Go 36:15 Closing Praise
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/8747

item.title: Guided Meditation: Emotions and Inner Safety
item.description:
Our problems often feel solid because they rest on a conclusion, and this guided meditation keeps returning to the place before the conclusion hardens. The practice moves through the felt life of emotion in the body—pleasant, unpleasant, named or vague—asking for steady, gentle attention instead of clinging, bracing, or trying to solve everything at once. As the sit deepens, the talk widens into the relationship between body, mind, and the stories we build, using a brief story about a man in severe suffering to show how problems can look different in immediate awareness.
Chapters: 00:01 Opening reflection 07:11 Emotion in the body 10:39 Mindfulness and equanimity 17:05 Receiving emotion gently 24:32 Body and mind 29:22 Radical permission 37:34 After the bell 42:27 Where are my problems? 46:37 Closing encouragement
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/8743

item.title: Guided Meditation: Settling, Body, Breath
item.description:
How do you meet a meditation period that does not go the way you imagined? The talk begins by loosening the grip of expectation and then moves into a steady guided settling: relaxing the face and jaw, letting the shoulders drain, keeping the posture dignified but the belly soft, and resting more fully in the breath and body. From there it opens into walking practice, where precise attention to the feet, the speed of movement, boredom, and the pull toward distraction all become part of the training in continuity and ease.
Chapters: 00:00 Settling the mind 03:22 Modest responsibilities 04:47 Relax the face 11:09 Upright and soft 19:06 Making peace with imperfection 24:54 The breath at close range 40:04 Retreat as cooking 50:31 Walking and continuity
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/8739

item.title: Matthew Brensilver, Sylvia Boorstein: Day 2: Evening Practice Session (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
How do the words of the Metta Sutta become something you can actually live by? The talk moves from a quiet opening on the retreat ending, into a line-by-line reading of the sutta as moral training, mind training, and wisdom training, with pauses for partner discussion and reflection on phrases like “omitting none” and “may all beings be at ease.” It then widens into a longer meditation on kindness, carefulness, and the way small acts shape the next moment, ending with Martin Luther King Jr.’s language of loving enemies and refusing ill will without surrendering principled action.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Additional teachers: Sylvia Boorstein
Chapters: 01:30 Opening the evening 02:58 Reading the text together 15:44 Metta as instruction 20:00 Pair work and daily use 28:57 Repetition and reflection 58:16 Suffering and consequence 01:13:48 Benefits of metta 01:19:48 Enemies and freedom 01:23:34 Closing sit and walk
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/48356/

item.title: Matthew Brensilver, Sylvia Boorstein: Day 2: Afternoon Practice Session (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
What happens when the practice stops being a private project and starts changing the way a whole life is seen? The talk moves from a simple instruction in directed attention, through a vivid story about irritation in family life, into a broader reflection on how clarity cuts through editorializing and reveals what is actually happening. From there it widens further: renunciation, death, love, and the Eightfold Path are treated not as separate doctrines but as parts of one living pattern, ending in a closing walking practice of wishing well to everyone encountered.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Additional teachers: Sylvia Boorstein
Chapters: 00:00 Practice as attention 08:36 Widening the field 22:57 Retreat and first tears 44:51 Inching into Dharma 55:22 Perspective and mortality 01:06:56 Clarity and the heart 01:18:57 Renunciation and dying 01:29:55 The path in practice 01:35:08 Walking with goodwill
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/48355/

item.title: Matthew Brensilver, Sylvia Boorstein: Day 2: Morning Practice Session
item.description:
Start with the body, then keep widening the circle: this morning session moves from standing warm-ups and qigong into posture, sitting, and a long metta practice. Along the way, the talk keeps returning to one question: how do we stay present with sensation, discomfort, and changing mind states without turning away? The movement is quiet but practical, ending in phrases of goodwill that spread from self, to friend, to familiar stranger, to everyone in the room, and finally to all beings.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Additional teachers: Sylvia Boorstein

Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/48343/

item.title: Matthew Brensilver, Sylvia Boorstein: Day 1: Afternoon Practice Session (part 1) (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
What happens when the mind keeps stepping into old movies instead of staying with the breath, the body, and the present moment? The talk moves from a simple sitting instruction—resting in breath, hearing, and bodily feeling—into careful ways of working with distraction, pain, and emotional reactivity. It then widens into a quieter insight: practice is less about fixing every wound than learning how to soften around experience without hardening or getting lost in the realm.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Additional teachers: Sylvia Boorstein
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the mind 01:37 Walking toward clarity 03:31 Sitting instructions 22:27 Naming the breath 32:48 Body awareness cue 34:31 Working with pain 54:57 Emotional movie loops 01:00:08 Wounds and compassion 01:07:22 Out of the realm
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/48322/
item.itunes:author: Matthew Brensilver
item.itunes:summary:
What happens when the mind keeps stepping into old movies instead of staying with the breath, the body, and the present moment? The talk moves from a simple sitting instruction—resting in breath, hearing, and bodily feeling—into careful ways of working with distraction, pain, and emotional reactivity. It then widens into a quieter insight: practice is less about fixing every wound than learning how to soften around experience without hardening or getting lost in the realm.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Additional teachers: Sylvia Boorstein
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the mind 01:37 Walking toward clarity 03:31 Sitting instructions 22:27 Naming the breath 32:48 Body awareness cue 34:31 Working with pain 54:57 Emotional movie loops 01:00:08 Wounds and compassion 01:07:22 Out of the realm
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/48322/

item.title: Matthew Brensilver, Sylvia Boorstein: Day 1: Evening Practice Session (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
How does practice move from steady attention into a heart that can let go? The talk begins with simple instructions for sitting and walking meditation, then turns into a long, clear sweep through the Buddha’s story, the Four Noble Truths, and the Eightfold Path as a way of training out of confusion and into clarity. From there it opens into the everyday work of the path: patience, generosity, honesty, grief, and the softening that comes when possession gives way to gratitude.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Additional teachers: Sylvia Boorstein
Chapters: 00:00 Opening instructions 01:53 Shared practice 49:49 Buddhist basics 01:03:11 Practice stays human 01:13:18 Paramis and early inspiration 01:15:37 Compassion and non-clinging 01:18:24 Letting go of ownership 01:29:53 Freedom through giving 01:36:58 Moonlight and closing
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/48332/

item.title: Matthew Brensilver, Sylvia Boorstein: Day 1: Afternoon Practice Session (part 2) (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
How do you stay steady when the mind is irritated, tired, restless, or full of doubt? The talk begins with ordinary annoyances and then turns into a set of practical antidotes: noticing breath more keenly, using brief changes in breathing to wake up, and learning how to meet afflictive states without making them bigger. Along the way, the speaker uses poems, a snow globe image, and later a story about a woman still marching with oxygen and a wheelchair to show how practice can be both intimate and socially engaged.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Additional teachers: Sylvia Boorstein
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the weather 02:47 Annoyance and humor 05:51 Naming the trouble 09:25 Mindfulness as truce 13:33 Breath as antidote 21:21 Awakening attention 33:43 Resting in awareness 50:29 Compassion and courage 01:05:04 Practice and civic life
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/48328/

item.title: Matthew Brensilver, Sylvia Boorstein: Day 1: Morning Practice Session (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
How do you practice when life is intense, the mind keeps spinning stories, and even the wish for progress can become another trap? The talk moves from the convergence of mindfulness and loving-kindness into a steady, practical inquiry: how to soften, breathe, walk, sit, and meet experience without making enemies of it. Along the way, the speaker uses Daniel Kahneman, After Life, the Metta Sutta, and the Foundations of Mindfulness to show how awareness and openheartedness can be trained together. The second half becomes more conversational and embodied, with questions about control, dukkha, pleasure, restlessness, and the ordinary friction of retreat life.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Additional teachers: Sylvia Boorstein
Chapters: 00:00 Opening to practice 10:17 A moment to live forever 14:09 Equanimity and attention 44:51 Softening into the body 01:26:51 Walking with goodwill 01:42:36 Dukkha and hardening 02:31:54 Not to control life 03:01:43 Practice, food, and closing
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/48321/

item.title: Matthew Brensilver, Sylvia Boorstein: Opening Night: Evening Practice Session (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
How do you begin a retreat by making it feel less like an audience and more like a shared refuge? The talk starts with introductions, a short opening practice, and a simple instruction to meet each moment fully and as a friend, then gradually widens into the ethical frame of the weekend: quiet, careful speech, non-harming, and the precepts as conditions for happiness. Along the way, the speaker returns to the same practical question—what kind of response is actually helpful—using a Buddha story, a retreat container, and a few memorable phrases like “tranquil and alert” to point toward a steadier mind.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Additional teachers: Sylvia Boorstein
Chapters: 00:31 Welcoming the retreat 03:51 Retreat instructions 08:03 Seeing the people here 16:58 Meet it as a friend 39:15 Skillful response 45:37 Collective refuge 50:38 Precepts and safety 01:01:38 Portable practice phrases 01:06:26 Tranquil and alert
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/48314/

item.title: Guided Meditation: The Clouds are Sky Too
item.description:
What happens when meditation stops treating experience as a problem to solve and starts trusting awareness more than the object of attention? The talk moves from relaxing effort and asking what it means to be alive and not waiting for anything, into a longer reflection on safety, permission, and the way present-moment experience arises and passes without needing to be fought. It then deepens the practice image of awareness as sky and thoughts, feelings, sounds, and even the sense of self as clouds, before turning to a repeated Rumi refrain about diving into the ocean and letting the sea be you.
Chapters: 00:00 Settling with awareness 05:49 Not waiting anymore 10:09 Safety in the present 15:41 Permission to arise 25:34 Radical acceptance 31:54 Sky and clouds 37:55 Rumi's ocean image 41:00 Putting down effort
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/8129
