item.title: Day 5: Instructions and Sitting
item.description:
The talk begins with mindfulness as a kind of soft readiness, then quickly turns that image into a bodily practice: the mind relaxes, the belly relaxes, and the body becomes porous to the world. From there, the instruction keeps loosening effort—there is no need to perform awareness, no need to wait for the next breath, and no need to defend against the future. Near the end, the talk settles into a quiet encouragement to give yourself over more fully to the present, letting go of past and future, self and world, as the bell approaches.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Soft readiness 00:40 Settling the body 07:22 No need to perform 09:01 Letting the moment be 20:31 Time and safety 22:32 Returning to sitting 34:23 Final release
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/41171/

item.title: Day 4: Metta Sit with Guidance (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
How do we meet another person’s good fortune without immediately measuring ourselves against it? The talk begins with a simple story about a child’s fear of lions and turns toward mudita, sympathetic joy, as something that can arise naturally when the heart is less defended and less scarce. From there, the practice moves through phrases for a loved one, then turns carefully toward someone whose happiness feels threatening, using envy as a place to see self-clinging and soften around it. It ends by returning to tenderness for one’s own clinging and the relief of letting other people’s well-being mean no threat at all.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Opening into practice 02:59 What mudita means 04:35 Meeting envy 07:11 Envy as teacher 08:30 Settling the body 12:59 Joy for a dear person 19:51 Turning toward challenge 24:01 Working with comparison 29:53 Opening the heart
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/41144/

item.title: Day 3 Instructions and Sitting (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
How do emotional sensations and internal talk shape experience, and what changes when attention can stay with them without bracing or trying to fix them? The talk begins with very simple attention on breath or sound, then widens to the bodily fingerprints of feeling—pleasant, unpleasant, diffuse, or localized—and to the way thoughts can be heard as inner speech. It ends by bringing feeling and internal talk together, showing their reciprocal pull and inviting a steadier, more patient way of knowing whatever arises.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Simple Attention 05:47 Not Bracing 09:02 From Breath to Feeling 11:37 Body of Emotion 16:14 Rolling Tides 23:37 Equanimity and Softening 24:37 Listening to Thought 30:24 Hearing the Narrator 33:08 Feeling and Talk Together
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/41139/

item.title: Day 1: Instructions and Guided Meditation (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
How do you stay simple with the breath when thought, feeling, and old habits keep pulling attention away? The talk lays out a framework for practice in three movements: steadier attention, a calmer relationship to emotion, and a clearer sense of how experience is shaped by many causes rather than by a fixed self. From there it turns into a guided meditation that starts with relaxing the body, softening the face and spine, and gently returning again and again to the breath—sometimes with a quiet mental note of in and out.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the Retreat Day 02:44 Three Ways Mindfulness Works 05:15 Training Attention 08:59 Meeting Emotion Clearly 11:45 From Identity to Awareness 14:22 Settling the Body 20:58 Opening to Breath 25:42 Gentle Noting 31:01 Sustaining Simplicity
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/41098/

item.title: Guided Meditation: Breath
item.description:
Begin with the breath as a way of meeting experience without dividing it into right and wrong. The talk slowly settles into a simple practice: simplifying attention, feeling the inhale and exhale, using gentle notes like “in” and “out” only to come closer to the sensations themselves. As the meditation unfolds, other thoughts and feelings are acknowledged as present but not urgent, and the invitation becomes to return with patience, forgiveness, and then soften into a wider awareness of the whole body.
Chapters: 00:00 Arriving Kindly 03:39 Simplifying Attention 06:31 Breath as Object 10:38 Noting In and Out 16:18 Returning Gently 22:05 Winds of Experience 27:15 Letting Go Wider
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/7544

item.title: Morning instructions - Breath and Body
item.description:
What if the first task is not to fix experience, but to meet it with steadiness, kindness, and a little less judgment? The talk begins by naming the roughness of early retreat—aches, fatigue, distraction—and then keeps returning to a simple instruction: rest attention on the breath, especially the exhale when the system feels overstimulated, and come back gently whenever the mind wanders. From there, it widens into a practice of inclusion, where sounds, thoughts, discomfort, and silence are all allowed to arise while the breath stays in the foreground; midway through, it turns to walking meditation as a formal, embodied continuation of the same mindfulness.
Chapters: 00:01 Opening the retreat 01:30 Discomfort and conditioning 03:31 Kindness and simplicity 08:29 Working with the exhale 13:32 Breath counting practice 20:05 Including everything 26:05 Rilke and persistence 31:36 Settling into silence 37:13 Noticing without judging
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/7181

item.title: Guided Meditation (Day 6): Poems, Reflections, Silence
item.description:
The talk begins with sorrow and weight: a heavy burden may be seen as empty, but that does not make it light. From there, the practice movement is simple and steady—carry what is hard, open the heart to it, and let moment-by-moment life soften rather than harden the heart. After long stretches of silence, the teaching turns toward pain being redeemed through love and understanding, then ends with a more urgent note of wholehearted surrender: don’t stop short, dive in, and let the sea be you.
Chapters: 02:00 Sorrow and Emptiness 05:02 Open the Heart 08:44 Lord of Song Refrain 18:18 Softening the Heart 25:24 Pain Redeemed 34:29 Rumi's Invitation 35:44 Closing Silence
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/6630

item.title: Guided Meditation (Day 4): Emotion in the Body
item.description:
What happens when emotion is treated not as a problem to solve, but as something to feel in the body? The talk begins by turning attention away from projects and plans toward the immediacy of experience, then guides a slow practice of resting with the body’s emotional signals—often felt in the face, throat, chest, and belly. It moves through noting, softening effort, and meeting pleasant, unpleasant, and neutral experience with relaxed, alert awareness. A brief reference to Shinzen Young’s line about suffering and freedom sharpens the teaching: the task is not to go elsewhere, but to stay with this life as it is appearing now.
Chapters: 00:01 Settling Into Life 02:09 Opening the Heart 07:48 Suffering and Presence 14:40 Emotion in the Body 18:35 Finding the Sensation 22:14 Resting With Change 24:44 Relaxing Effort 28:52 Gentle Noting 30:38 Equanimity and Sky
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/6625

item.title: Guded Meditation (Day 2): Breath
item.description:
This guided meditation begins with settling the body through the breath, then asks what the mind brings to stillness and silence as the sit unfolds. The practice keeps returning to breathing in a gentle, matter-of-fact way, with repeated stretches of silence and reminders not to grasp tightly at the breath. The talk also turns to the strain of always trying to improve experience, and suggests opening to imperfection, letting thought and feeling pass without acting on their urgency, and relaxing the body-mind so the breath can stay in the foreground.
Chapters: 00:00 Settling In 04:56 Breath as Receiving 11:15 The Cost of Striving 13:30 Meeting Imperfection 25:56 Foreground and Background 32:01 Freedom from Urgency 41:56 After the Practice 46:13 Returning in Daily Life
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/6623

item.title: Morning Instructions - Awareness and Objects
item.description:
The talk begins with the habit of trying to get somewhere, then gently turns toward relaxing that effort and offering awareness to whatever is already here, with no strings attached. From there it follows a simple practice movement: notice what’s present, let it be known, and trust that this shift from identification to knowing brings a quiet kind of freedom. In the later discussion, the talk returns to how language can help the mind wake up from being caught in fear, envy, or self-story and settle into direct experience.
Chapters: 00:00 The Striving Mind 01:58 Relaxing Into Practice 05:45 No Strings Attached 14:00 Courage and Heart 20:40 Simple, Relaxed, Wise 31:18 Questions on Study 37:21 Being Known 39:03 Language and Right View 44:06 Closing the Retreat Day
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/6348

item.title: Guided Meditation - Awareness and Relaxation
item.description:
What if suffering feels like being backed into a corner—and the practice is to discover that the corner is not the whole room? This guided meditation moves gently from the body and breath to the simple act of knowing thought, sensation, emotion, and sound. The talk returns again and again to the idea that mindfulness does not erase pain, but can keep pain from having the last word by showing that something else is also true.
Chapters: 00:01 Practice Framing 01:03 Suffering and Release 02:40 More Than the Corner 04:00 Knowing What Arises 05:15 Settling Into Refuge 10:43 Returning Again 25:04 Trailing Audio
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/6346

item.title: Guided Meditation: Mindfulness of Mind
item.description:
What happens when the mind’s need to explain, label, and secure experience is gently met with silence? The talk moves through a guided resting in breath, body, emotion, images, and words, then turns toward seeing those formations arise and pass rather than believing they define the ground we stand on. Again and again, it returns to the possibility that it is safe not to know, and that awareness can hold what the thinking mind keeps trying to organize.
Chapters: 00:00 Settling In 02:25 Fresh Seeing 07:50 Breath Body Emotion 10:03 Trusting Not Knowing 13:16 The Mind's Security 19:29 Images And Words 25:02 Silence As Ground 31:22 Stepping Off 39:13 Closing Silence
Source: AudioDharma

What happens when the mind’s need to explain, label, and secure experience is gently met with silence? The talk moves through a guided resting in breath, body, emotion, images, and words, then turns toward seeing those formations arise and pass rather than believing they define the ground we stand on. Again and again, it returns to the possibility that it is safe not to know, and that awareness can hold what the thinking mind keeps trying to organize.

item.title: Walking Meditation
item.description:
Walking practice is framed here as a way to notice the mind’s hunger to get somewhere, even when the body is just moving back and forth on a short path. The talk follows the sensations of the feet, the pull of boredom or scatteredness, and the way visual impressions can stir association and pull attention away. It closes with a gentle reminder to keep the practice light: important, but not self-serious.
Chapters: 00:09 Welcome and sign-up 00:55 What walking practice is 01:56 Going nowhere 02:43 Reaching down 03:05 Seeing and association 03:58 Feet and toe 04:30 Practice without strain 04:48 Bell and closing
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/4495

item.title: Guided Meditation
item.description:
What happens when anger is no longer treated as one solid block, but as a mix of body sensation, inner talk, and mental images? The talk begins by laying out that question, drawing on a Buddhist map of experience and a three-part breakdown associated with Shinzen Young, then moves into a guided meditation that asks listeners to notice those strands separately. The practice stays close to direct experience: settling the body, sensing emotional energy, hearing thought as sound, and seeing images arise and pass away, so anger can feel more workable and less overwhelming.
Chapters: 00:12 Welcome and purpose 01:21 Breaking experience apart 03:31 How anger tangles 06:45 More manageability 08:23 Settling into practice 14:27 Feeling in the body 23:25 Listening to thought 29:17 Images and integration
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/4040
